entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 3 - JMS in Production
entry.content:
@HaraldBoeing we do use the XI adapter for two interfaces here, but that is relatively sparing considering the few dozen integration points that are in place, and the only real reason is to make use of the attachment handling that is supported.  Two file based interfaces where the file part becomes decoupled by making use of the attachment handling, but still guaranteeing delivery because XI makes use of the JMS adapter.

Regarding EOIO, I have yet to encounter a single legitimate instance in my 18+ years of working in the integration space within SAP of an interface truly needing that functionality - seems to me more an artifact of very outdated practices that have never truly matured or been modernized.

entry.title: Join Our Live Session April 8, 2026: Agent-Assisted Coding with SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Today’s fast-paced development environments demand smart, efficient solutions that integrate seamlessly into enterprise workflows. On April 8, 2026, join our live session: “Agent-Assisted Coding with AI Core” to learn how SAP AI Core’s Generative AI Hub can empower agent-assisted coding.

In this highly interactive 45-minute session, our experts will demonstrate how AI coding agents can autonomously generate, refactor, and test code while aligning with your architecture requirements. You will also gain insights into extending agent capabilities using the Model Context Protocol (MCP), turning AI agents into reliable, context-aware development partners.

No matter your experience level, this session is designed to equip developers with the tools and strategies to enhance productivity and innovation.

Key Highlights of the Live Session

entry.title: Modfiy Entities EML for creating records of only child entitiy does not work
entry.content:
This is regarding using standard RAP BO R_FunctionalLocationTP to create records in my se38 regular abap program.

The scenario is that already functional location exists in the standard db table IFLOT (W335-101HB-A001-S4) . But i want to create a class type as 0003 which is a child/associated entity of the RAP BO using EML in my SE38 program.

data: lt_floc_class type table for create r_functionallocationtp\_classification,
          lt_floc_class_char type table for create R_FuncnlLocClfnClassTP\_characteristic.

    lt_floc_class = value #( ( FunctionalLocation = 'W335-101HB-A001-S4'

                               %target = value #( (
                                                    FunctionalLocation = 'W335-101HB-A001-S4'
                                                     "ClassInternalID = ''
                                                     ClassType = '003'
                                                     "ChangeNumber = ''
                                                     %control-ClassType = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                      ) )

                           ) ).

    modify "entities of r_functionallocationtp
      entity R_FunctionalLocationTP
      create by \_classification
      auto fill cid with lt_floc_class
       mapped data(ls_mapped)
       failed data(ls_failed)
       reported data(ls_reported).

    commit entities
     response of R_FunctionalLocationTP
     failed final(lt_commit_failed)
     reported final(lt_commit_reported).

The above code is not creating the class type in KSSK table/IL03 table and in debugging there is no record in failed itab so I am not able to identify where is the issue.

entry.title: Re: Cloud ALM Documents – External File configuration mis...
entry.content:
Hi, Tian,

In order to upload external files for use in Cloud ALM, your are required to configure a connection to DMS.  Entitlement to this should be checked with your SAP Account Executive.  

I believe the page: Connecting SAP Document Management Service | SAP Help Portal will be helpful to you in making this configuration.  Also, see KBA 3696054 - How to configure Document Management Service to allow Document upload from Cloud ALM.

Best regards,
Paula

entry.title: Re: Latest People Profile in the H1 Release: Concerns and Request for Delay until H2 Release
entry.content: Hi, I completely agree. The more I test, the more defects and issues I continue to uncover. I’m dreading having to inform HR about the removal of the 9 box and the inability for others (with proper permissions) to view Learning History on an employee’s profile. This functionality was taken away years ago, then restored after multiple enhancement requests - and now we are losing it again. I’m also finding new issues related to RBP. When the profile category is granted, several sections display “no data,” which is not how this functions today in my Prod instance (KB3722226 & KB3724571). Pay Targets remain visible even after removing visibility in RBP (KB3710879), with a fix not planned until 1H 2026. I’m attempting to clean up RBP while I’m already making extensive updates, but it’s difficult when the system isn’t functioning correctly. My bigger concern is how I’m expected to update all of our system documentation and training materials when the test site isn’t yet working well enough to use for creating documentation, videos, or other training materials. Ideally, I would roll everything out at once, including the latest version of editing and history - but there are still too many issues to enable it. It is simply not fully functional. Initially, I was more optimistic, but the more I test and review, the more discouraged I become. In my honest opinion, this is not ready for a universal rollout. I am already navigating significant change management challenges with the new Onboarding version and its ongoing issues. I cannot take on additional backlash from managers, employees, and HR over yet another forced rollout outside of my control. Please give customers more time - at least until these issues are fully resolved.

entry.title: Access CDS View data through SAP Integration Suite for Power BI / Excel without embedding credential
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I am trying to expose data from an SAP CDS View through SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) so that it can be consumed from Power BI and Excel. It is compulsory to use Integration Suite.

The main constraint of the scenario is authentication.

The Power BI and Excel files will be shared with multiple users, so I cannot embed credentials (for example in VBA scripts or connection strings).
Instead, the idea is to expose the CDS data through Integration Suite as a controlled endpoint.

The desired architecture is:

 
Power BI / Excel
↓
SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration)
↓
SAP Gateway OData Service (generated from CDS View)
 

The CDS View is already published as an OData service in SAP Gateway, and it works correctly when accessed directly.

What I have tried so far

I tried two different approaches in Integration Suite.

1. OData → OData Integration Flow

First I created an integration flow using OData adapters on both sides.

Configuration:

Sender adapter

OData V2
Operation: Query (GET)

Receiver adapter

 

OData V2
Resource path: <EntitySet>
Query options: ${header.CamelHttpQuery}
Authentication: Basic (credential stored in Integration Suite)

Goal: create a transparent OData proxy.

Result:

  • The iFlow deployed successfully.

  • I was able to retrieve the metadata from the CDS view through this approach.

  • PowerBI detected the Set BUT gave Internal Server Error (Error no500), so I could not access the info (same problem if tried in browser).

2. HTTPS Sender → HTTP Receiver proxy

I then attempted a (supposely) simpler HTTP proxy approach.

Sender:

HTTPS
Address:
/powerbi/cds02/*
 
Integration flow:
HTTPS Sender
↓
Start
↓
Content Modifier
↓
HTTP Receiver

Content Modifier headers:

CamelHttpPath = ${header.CamelHttpPath}
CamelHttpQuery = ${header.CamelHttpQuery}
 

The idea was to dynamically forward the request path and query string.
In SAP Cloud Integration, the CamelHttpQuery header contains the query parameters from the original request URL.

Receiver configuration:

Address: https://<backend>/sap/opu/odata/sap/<SERVICE_NAME>
Authentication: Basic

Result:

  • The iFlow deploys successfully.

  • However every request returns: An internal server error occured: The MPL ID for the failed message is : (number)

So in practice this HTTP proxy approach does not reach the CDS data at all.

Current status

Summary of what I achieved so far:

Approach Result
OData → ODataMetadata reached (column names got), but not data in it (internal server error)
HTTPS → HTTP proxyRequests fail with internal server error

Questions

  1. What is the recommended architecture to expose CDS data through Integration Suite for consumption by Power BI / Excel?

  2. Is using Integration Suite as an OData proxy the correct approach in this scenario?

  3. Are there special considerations for handling $metadata and query parameters when forwarding OData requests through CPI?

  4. Is there a better approach to solve the authentication constraint (no embedded credentials in Excel / Power BI files)?

Any guidance or examples of similar implementations would be greatly appreciated. I could not even find any tutorials or anything (involving Integration Suite), though I guess it is something used in many companies (it is my first time doing this).

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: Replicate the QR Code to custom Smartform, decoding from Table EDOPLINV
entry.content:
Hi All,

How to replicate the QR Code, for an Invoice that was accepted by the KSeF, e-invoicing System and sent back the PDF with QR Code?  The QR Code (not the pdf)  is stored in the Table Field EDOPLINV-QRCODE_ACCEPTED.

Expected Result: 
The QR Code should be re-generated in a custom Smart form, from the value stored in the Table field EDOPLINV-QRCODE_ACCEPTED. when scanned, it should re-direct to KSef PL Gov website.

Procedure:
I have the following test code written in the Smart form, very similar to this blog post QR Code in Base64 encoding for KSA E-Invoicing - Directly from data in Table EDOSAINV  
Note: In the- above Blog, QR Code is  encoded to Base 64, But I don't want this.

  1. Configured System Bar Code in SE73 for QR Code with "QR Code 2005".
  2. Created a smart form style, For a  Character font, assigned the QR Code system bar code to it.
  3. Assigned the custom style to my custom Smart form.
  4. Below is the code written in the Initialization of Smart form (Please note this is a prototype)
*Global Data
 Data:
 EV_QRCODE type string. "or xstring
SELECT SINGLE
  FROM edoplinv
FIELDS *
 WHERE edoc_guid = '699D630206330553E1000000A467BBA9'
  INTO ls_edoplinv.

  DATA lv_image_base64 TYPE string.
  DATA lo_xstr_to_str_conv TYPE REF TO cl_abap_conv_in_ce.
  cl_abap_conv_in_ce=>create(
    EXPORTING
      encoding = 'UTF-8'
      input    = ls_edoplinv-qr_code_accepted
    RECEIVING
      conv     = lo_xstr_to_str_conv
  ).
  lo_xstr_to_str_conv->read( IMPORTING data = lv_image_base64 ).

  DATA: lv_image_xstring TYPE xstring.
  lv_image_xstring = cl_http_utility=>decode_x_base64( 
                         encoded = lv_image_base64 ).

*--Assign to the QRCode variable used in the UI
   EV_QRCODE = lv_image_xstring

In the smart form, created a text with the following snippet

<QR>&EV_QRCODE&</>​

The QR Code is generated, but does not point to the KSeF, instead shows a 4 digit gibberish value.

Expected URL starts with below URL
https://qr-demo.ksef.mf.gov/client-app/invoice/

 Can you please suggest how to fix it ?

entry.title: Re: Create new/custom PO Header or Item Text in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Ruby,
thanks for your question.

Short answer

Yes – in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, the recommended way to add a custom PO header or item text and make it visible in the Fiori PO apps is to use key‑user extensibility via the “Custom Fields” app (Custom Fields & Logic). You work with the Procurement: Purchasing Document and Procurement: Purchasing Document Item business contexts and then enable the field on the relevant UIs and (optionally) APIs. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
Because your PO is not printed and is only stored for reference, you do not need to touch forms/output management – you can keep everything in the Fiori and API layer.

Recommended approach (header & item text visible in PO Fiori apps)

Below is a generic pattern that works for the standard PO apps in S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

1. Create the custom field in the correct business context

  1. Log on to the SAP Fiori launchpad as an administrator / key user.
  2. Open the Custom Fields app (in the Extensibility group). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Create a new custom field:
    • Choose + (Add).
    • For a header‑level PO text, use business context
      • Procurement: Purchasing Document. [help.sap.com]
    • For an item‑level PO text, use business context
      • Procurement: Purchasing Document Item. [help.sap.com]
    • Choose a suitable field type, for example:
      • Text with appropriate length (for short notes), or
      • Long Text / Text Area if you want a longer, free‑form comment field (depending on what’s offered in your release).
  4. Maintain the basic properties (label, tooltip, length, etc.) and save the draft.
The external documentation confirms that key users can extend the purchase order apps using exactly these business contexts for header and item level. [help.sap.com]

2. Enable the custom field in the relevant PO UIs (Fiori apps)

After the field is created, you must explicitly enable it for the Fiori apps:
  1. In Custom Fields, open your newly created field again.
  2. Go to the UIs and Reports tab. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Look for the PO‑related data sources, for example (names may vary slightly by release):
    • Purchase Order GUI Application (header)
    • Purchase Order Item GUI (item) [help.sap.com]
    • Plus any specific Fiori apps you use such as:
      • Manage Purchase Orders
      • Create Purchase Order – Advanced (if applicable) [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  4. Tick the checkbox to enable your field for these UIs.
  5. Publish the custom field.
According to the PO extensibility documentation, once you have enabled the custom fields for the purchase‑order‑related data sources in the UIs and Reports section, the fields appear in the Custom Fields section of the PO header and/or item details. [help.sap.com]
After publishing, users will typically see your field:
  • In the header details → “Custom Fields” or similar subsection.
  • In the item details → “Custom Fields” tab/section at item level.

3. (Optional) Enable the custom field for APIs used to replicate the PO from the external system

Because your POs are coming from an external system and only stored in S/4HANA Cloud for reference, you likely also want this custom text field to be available in the integration layer.
  1. Stay in the same custom field in the Custom Fields app.
  2. Still under UIs and Reports, look for API / data source entries, for example:
    • Purchase Order (OData V4) – this API explicitly supports extension fields added via the Custom Fields app. [help.sap.com]
  3. Enable the field for the relevant API and re‑publish.
The Purchase Order (OData V4) API documentation states that you can add extension fields to the API via the Custom Fields app and Custom Logic app (Key User Extensibility). [help.sap.com]
If you are using the asynchronous SOAP API PurchaseOrderRequest_In to create or update POs from your external system, you can review that service as well and align the mapping so that your external field (from the source system) is mapped into the new custom field in S/4HANA Cloud. [help.sap.com]

4. Use (optional) custom logic for defaults or validations

If you need additional behavior around the new text, you can use key‑user BAdIs via Custom Logic:
  1. Open the Custom Logic app. [help.sap.com]
  2. Search for PO‑related enhancement options (BAdIs) and create an implementation that:
    • Sets a default value for your text based on other header or item data, or
    • Performs checks/validations (e.g., require a comment if some condition is met).
The general extensibility guide explains that BAdIs can be implemented via Custom Logic to adapt app behavior, including additional checks or default values, and they work nicely in combination with custom fields. [help.sap.com]

5. Governance / operations tip

From a project and operations perspective, it’s a good practice to document all extensibility objects (custom fields, logic, CDS views, etc.) so you understand their impact on future releases and upgrades. This is a general recommendation for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition projects: every change to extensibility objects should be tracked and documented.
This is especially relevant in your scenario where POs are replicated from another system – it ensures everyone knows how the custom text is filled, mapped, and displayed.

Notes / constraints

  • Edition & scope: The above is valid for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition and its PO Fiori apps using the key‑user extensibility framework (Custom Fields app). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • No change to standard PO types needed: Adding such a field is done via extensibility. You should not need to change standard PO document type configuration to achieve this. (There is a general recommendation not to change standard PO/PR document type configuration, but to use copies if you need different behavior.)
  • Output / printing: Since you explicitly said the PO will not be printed, there is no need to enable the field on forms or email templates. If in future you decide to print, you can also enable the custom field for form templates from the same Custom Fields app. [help.sap.com]

Sources

  • Extensibility for Purchase Order Apps – business contexts and how custom fields appear in PO header/item custom fields sections [help.sap.com]
  • Custom Fields app – overview & procedure – how to create and publish custom fields, enable them for UIs/APIs/templates [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • Purchase Order (OData V4) API – support for adding extension fields via Custom Fields & Logic [help.sap.com]
  • PurchaseOrderRequest_In (SOAP) – inbound purchase order service for external systems [help.sap.com]
  • Adaptation of App Behavior / Custom Logic app – using BAdIs for additional checks, defaulting, mappings with custom fields [help.sap.com]
  • Project recommendation: keep track of every change made to extensibility objects – general best practice for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition projects

AI transparency note

Note: This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance like SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C) (see <https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/sap-consulting-capability.html>) and then reviewed for source support. Please validate against the linked official documentation for your specific release.
 
If this helps, please mark the answer as accepted so others can benefit as well. Thank you.

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition] Migrating from Third-...
entry.content:
Thanks for the question, Cristian.
 
Short answer:
Yes, migrating from a customer‑managed CMIS repository (e.g. OpenText via BTP DMS / CMIS) to the SAP‑managed “default” BTP Document Management Service repository is conceptually possible, but there is no one‑click or fully automated “in‑place migration” described for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition. The two guides you found both describe the technical migration of a CMIS repository to SAP DMS, not an S/4HANA‑specific end‑to‑end procedure. You’ll need to combine SAP’s generic CMIS‑to‑SAP DMS guidance with S/4HANA Cloud’s CMIS profile and repository configuration.
Below is how I’d structure the approach and how to interpret those two documents.

1. Clarify the current setup

From your description, you likely have:
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition using Attachment Service
  • Attachment Service is connected to an external CMIS‑compatible repository (OpenText) via BTP / CMIS
  • You now want to move to the SAP‑managed BTP Document Management Service (DMS) repository, which is the default option for S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.
The key is: S/4HANA Cloud does not store documents itself; it stores references (links/IDs) to objects in the CMIS repository. The migration must therefore preserve:
  • The physical documents
  • The CMIS structure and metadata that S/4HANA Cloud expects (SAP CMIS Profile)
  • The IDs / paths in such a way that S/4HANA can still resolve existing attachments after you switch repositories.

2. What the two migration guides actually cover

You mentioned:
  1. “Migrating from Third‑Party CMIS Repository” | SAP Help Portal
  2. “Migrating Third‑Party CMIS Repository to SAP Document Management Service” | SAP Help Portal
These documents are generic for BTP Document Management Service, not specific to S/4HANA Cloud, but they are the right starting point for your case. They typically describe:
  • How to export content from a third‑party CMIS repository
  • How to transform and import it into a BTP DMS repository
  • How to map folder structure, metadata and permissions in a way that SAP applications can consume
In parallel, for S/4HANA Cloud you must follow the documentation for Third‑Party Repository Integration with S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS and the SAP CMIS Profile, which explains how S/4HANA expects business objects and attachments to appear in the CMIS repository. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
So:
  • The migration guides tell you how to move CMIS content into SAP DMS.
  • The S/4HANA Cloud + CMIS docs tell you how the repository must look so that S/4HANA continues to work.
You’ll likely need both sets of documentation.

3. Recommended high‑level migration approach

A possible high‑level plan (conceptual, to be refined for your customer):
  1. Analyse existing CMIS implementation (OpenText)
    • Review how the existing repository implements the SAP CMIS Profile (folder structure, object types, properties like business object keys, content types, etc.). [help.sap.com]
    • Identify which business object types (e.g. FI documents, sales documents, purchase orders) are in scope.
  2. Provision the SAP‑managed BTP DMS repository
    • Set up a SAP Document Management Service, repository option (SAP‑managed) instance as per BTP DMS documentation.
    • Ensure it is configured with CMIS support and that it can represent the SAP CMIS Profile structures required by S/4HANA Cloud. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  3. Perform CMIS‑level content migration
    • Use the guidance in the two “Third‑Party CMIS Repository migration” documents to:
      • Export content + metadata from OpenText CMIS.
      • Map and import into the SAP DMS repository, preserving:
        • Folder hierarchy / structure expected by the SAP CMIS Profile
        • Object properties (business object keys, document types, etc.)
        • Versioning and, where possible, permissions.
    • The critical requirement is that, after migration, the new SAP DMS repository exposes the same CMIS object IDs or a consistent mapping that S/4HANA can use when resolving existing attachments. The official documentation describes how S/4HANA Cloud uses CMIS APIs to locate content in the repository; make sure your migration doesn’t break that mapping. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  4. Reconfigure S/4HANA Cloud to point to the new repository
    • In S/4HANA Cloud, update the Third‑Party Repository Integration configuration so that the Attachment Service now points to the SAP DMS repository instead of the OpenText CMIS endpoint. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
    • This step is where any mismatch of IDs or structure will surface: if the repository content is migrated 1:1 at CMIS level, S/4HANA should be able to resolve existing attachments without change.
  5. Test with sample business objects first
    • Before switching the entire landscape, test for a few representative objects:
      • Open existing FI document, sales order, purchase order, etc.
      • Check that attachments are still visible and that new uploads are stored in the SAP‑managed DMS repository.
    • If tests show broken links, you may need an additional mapping layer or a repair script at the CMIS level (this is not covered out‑of‑the‑box in generic documentation and would be a project‑specific activity).
  6. Plan cut‑over and fall‑back
    • Schedule a cut‑over window where:
      • You first freeze changes (no document uploads) in the old repository.
      • Complete the final delta migration into SAP DMS.
      • Switch S/4HANA Cloud config to the new repository.
    • Keep the old repository read‑only for a period as a fall‑back and for forensic checks.

4. Which of the two guides is the “right one”?

Because these are BTP DMS‑level guides (not S/4HANA‑Cloud‑specific), the “right” guide depends on the exact implementation, but in practice you will:
  • Use “Migrating from Third‑Party CMIS Repository” as the conceptual migration overview (how to handle CMIS export/import, structure, and metadata).
  • Use “Migrating Third‑Party CMIS Repository to SAP Document Management Service” as the concrete procedure for moving from a CMIS‑based repository to BTP Document Management Service (SAP‑managed).
In other words, they are complementary rather than mutually exclusive. The S/4HANA‑specific behavior is governed by:
  • Third‑Party Repository Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS (Attachment Service + CMIS integration pattern), and
  • SAP CMIS Profile (how business objects are represented and consumed in CMIS repositories). [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]

5. Important constraints and caveats

Because there is no single S/4HANA Cloud “wizard” that migrates attachments between repositories, please keep in mind:
  • The migration is essentially a content + metadata migration at CMIS/BTP level.
  • Preserving or remapping CMIS IDs and business object references is critical for attachment continuity; this part is typically handled by a custom migration tool or script, not by S/4HANA itself.
  • Any customizations done in OpenText CMIS (custom properties, folder concepts, retention) need to be evaluated and mapped into SAP DMS equivalents where possible.
  • Always validate your approach in a test tenant / test BTP subaccount before doing anything in production.

6. References

  • Third‑Party Repository Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud via CMIS (Attachment Service) – SAP Help Portal [help.sap.com]
  • SAP CMIS Profile – how S/4HANA Cloud represents business objects in CMIS repositories [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • CMIS APIs Invoked from SAP S/4HANA Cloud – which CMIS operations S/4HANA uses [help.sap.com]
  • General SAP S/4HANA Cloud Help Portal entry for latest integration docs [help.sap.com]
 
AI transparency note
Note: This draft was prepared with AI‑powered assistance like SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C) and then aligned with the official documentation references above. Please validate the final approach and details against the Help Portal for your specific S/4HANA Cloud release.
 
If this helped you, please consider marking the answer as accepted so others can find it as well.

entry.title: BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE returns success message but does not take effect.
entry.content:
I am trying to use the BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE inside MV45AFZZ and SE37 but the changes does not reflect on the documents.

Even though there was a message that the Sales Document was saved, the changes does not reflect when i check it in VA03 or SE16.

DATA: ls_order_header_in  TYPE bapisdh1,
      ls_order_header_inx TYPE bapisdh1x,
      lt_return           TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.
CLEAR: 
     ls_order_header_in, 
     ls_order_header_inx, 
     lt_return.

IF vbak-auart = 'ZVCC'.

SELECT 
       vbeln 
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_vbfa)
  FROM vbfa
  WHERE vbelv = vbak-vbeln
    AND vbtyp_n = 'C'.

IF lt_vbfa IS NOT INITIAL.

" Some contract have related multiple sales doc
LOOP AT lt_vbfa INTO DATA(ls_vbfa).
  ls_order_header_in-incotermsv = vbak-incov.
  ls_order_header_inx-updateflag = 'U'.
  ls_order_header_inx-incotermsv = abap_true.

  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE'
    EXPORTING
      salesdocument    = ls_vbfa-vbeln
      order_header_in  = ls_order_header_in
      order_header_inx = ls_order_header_inx
    TABLES
      return           = lt_return.
ENDLOOP.
ENDIF.
ENDIF.

entry.title: Re: Component Customisation - EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF error
entry.content:
Hi Chris,
thanks for the detailed explanation and the screenshot.
From your description and the error list, two things stand out:
  • You’re in the WDA configuration for component EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF (Change Maintenance Order).
  • Even before you apply your actual change, you already see several FPM/Floorplan Manager errors, including:
    • “The name contains invalid characters, valid characters are A‑Z, 0‑9, _, and /”
    • “UIBB key is not valid (Component FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS, Config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG, Instance ID …)”
    • “Error at instantiation of component FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS with config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG”

1) First isolate / clean up the existing configuration errors

Before trying to hide Create Follow‑on Order, I’d try to verify whether these errors are:
  • Already present in the standard configuration, or
  • Introduced only when you start working with your custom configuration (copy / Z‑config).
Some checks you can run:
  1. Open the standard configuration again without any custom copy
    • If the same “UIBB key is not valid” and “invalid characters” errors already appear, then there may be an inconsistency in the delivered configuration, and that needs to be clarified first (e.g. via internal analysis or SAP channels), before doing any enhancement.
    • If the standard config is clean and the errors only show after working with your own config ID, then the issue is very likely in your custom configuration name or in a referenced UIBB key.
  2. Re‑check the IDs you entered manually
    • The error text explicitly says: “valid characters are A‑Z, 0‑9, _, and /”.
    • Double‑check that:
      • The configuration ID you are using (and any child UIBB configuration IDs) only use these characters, and
      • There is no leading/trailing blank or hidden character in the field (sometimes this can trigger “invalid name” symptoms).
  3. Verify the UIBB configuration actually exists and is active
    • For FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS with config ID EAMS3_WDC_DIR_LINK_LI_CFG, check in the FPM / Web Dynpro configuration tools that:
      • This configuration is present,
      • It is active, and
      • It is reachable from your main component configuration (no typo in the key).
If the UIBB configuration is missing or inactive, the framework will typically raise exactly the sort of “UIBB key is not valid” instantiation errors you are seeing.

2) Approaches to hide “Create Follow‑on Order” in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Your functional goal is to prevent users from creating YA02 follow‑on orders from this WDA app, because document type restrictions don’t fully protect you.
On S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in general, the recommended direction is to prefer standard configuration, authorizations, and clean‑core extensibility over deep UI framework changes. 
With that in mind, you basically have these options:
  1. Authorization / role-based approach (if there is a suitable auth check behind the action)
    • If the Create Follow‑on Order action is tied to an authorization object or business role (e.g. users who cannot create YA02 at all), that is usually the cleanest solution.
    • In that case you’d:
      • Make sure only a dedicated role can create YA02, and
      • Remove that role from users who should not see/use this capability.
  2. UI-level hiding via configuration / personalization (if exposed by the framework)
    • In some WDA / FPM applications, toolbar or menu actions can be hidden through:
      • Configuration of the toolbar schema / action schema, or
      • Switching off the corresponding UIBB / action in the FPM configuration.
    • The errors you are seeing need to be resolved first, though – otherwise your configuration is in an inconsistent state and further changes may not behave predictably.
  3. Re‑evaluate whether a different, more “cloud‑aligned” app could be used
    • SAP’s guidance for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is to:
      • Use standard processes and apps as far as possible and
      • Limit heavy UI framework modifications, especially in classic technologies, to reduce upgrade risk and rework. 
    • If there is a Fiori app or alternative process where you can control the follow‑on creation more cleanly (e.g. via restrictions and app-level authorizations), that may be easier to maintain over time.

3) Suggested next steps you could try

Given what we can see from the screenshot only:
  1. Clarify the baseline:
    • Confirm whether the standard EAMS_WDA_ORDNTF_OIF configuration is error‑free.
    • If errors exist even in the standard, resolve those first.
  2. Check your custom config IDs / keys:
    • Ensure all IDs involved in your copied configuration (main component config + any UIBBs for FPM_LIST_UIBB_ATS) use only A‑Z, 0‑9, _ , / and actually exist.
    • Correct any typos or recreate the configuration if necessary.
  3. Once the config is clean, try hiding the action:
    • Look for the toolbar / action configuration entry that corresponds to Create Follow‑on Order and try setting it to “not visible” (or remove it from the schema), if the framework allows that.
    • After the change, test with a business user role to make sure the option is really gone and there are no side effects.
If you can share whether you copied the configuration or changed the standard, the community might be able to give more targeted hints or screenshots of a working config.
Note on AI assistance
This answer was prepared with the help of an AI assistant based on publicly available SAP guidance and your question text, and should be double‑checked against your system configuration and official SAP documentation before applying in a customer environment. 
 
If this response helps you move forward, please consider marking it as accepted so others with a similar question can find it more easily.

entry.title: Re: Details of Customers' Certificates is made mandatory...
entry.content:
Thanks for the question, Ritesh.
 
Short summary
The “Certificate Details” sheet in the customer master migration template corresponds to India‑specific TCS (Tax Collected at Source) certificates. If your customer has no such certificates, you normally don’t have any business data to migrate there. You should still keep the sheet in the template (because it’s marked mandatory at template level) but you can test migrating with no data rows, and only maintain certificate records later in the “Customer: Ctry‑Spec. Enh.” app if and when they are needed.
Below is a more detailed explanation and a suggested approach.

1. What the “Certificate Details” fields are for

From the Performance Assistant help for Certificate Number (your screenshot), we know:
In India, there are certain goods on which Tax Collected at Source (TCS) is applicable. Businesses which trade in such goods can register with the government entity and receive a unique identifier (Certificate Number) in order to reduce TCS rate on such goods.
So, the “Certificate Details” sheet in the customer migration template is there to preload this India TCS certificate data into the customer master, similar to how certificate details are stored for other business partners and then used in checks and business functions. [help.sap.com]
If your Indian customers do not have such certificates, there’s simply no certificate data to migrate.

2. Why the sheet is marked as “mandatory” in the migration cockpit

In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition migration cockpit, each migration object has an Excel XML template with multiple sheets. Some sheets are flagged as mandatory, some as optional. [help.sap.com]
“Mandatory” here refers to the template structure:
  • The sheet itself must be present with its original name and headers.
  • It does not automatically mean that every field on the sheet must be filled for every customer. [help.sap.com]
Because this is an India‑specific view of the customer object, SAP has made the “Certificate Details” sheet mandatory in the template structure, but that doesn’t necessarily mean you must have at least one certificate record for every customer.

3. How to proceed if you have no certificate details from the customer

Given the above, a conservative and usually sufficient approach is:
  1. Keep the “Certificate Details” sheet in the template.
    • Don’t delete or rename it. Deleting mandatory sheets is exactly what causes template validation errors in the migration cockpit. [help.sap.com]
  2. Leave the sheet structurally intact but with no data rows if there are really no TCS certificates to migrate.
    • Keep all the delivered column headers as they are.
    • Do not enter any dummy certificate numbers unless you want them to appear as real records in the system.
  3. Upload the file via “Migrate Your Data” and run a test migration in a test or sandbox tenant. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
    • Check whether the migration object accepts the template with an empty “Certificate Details” sheet.
    • If the template imports successfully and no error is raised specifically about missing certificate rows, you can proceed in the same way in the productive migration.
  4. If (and only if) the cockpit explicitly demands at least one row in that sheet (for example, you get a validation error saying that data is required on the sheet), then:
    • Re‑check with your India tax/business process owner whether there are in fact any TCS certificates that should be captured.
    • If they confirm there are none, you have a design decision to make:
      • Either keep the sheet empty and raise the behavior with SAP (for example via Community or an influence request) if the migration insists on data where none should exist; or
      • Agree on a clearly documented placeholder convention, understanding that such placeholders will create master data that then has to be cleaned up later.
    • Because official documentation does not state that data rows are mandatory for this sheet, I would first try to keep it empty and treat any “data required” message as something to be further investigated rather than immediately creating dummy business data. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]

4. Maintaining certificates later (if needed)

If at some point your Indian customers do obtain TCS certificates, you can maintain them directly in the relevant Fiori apps (for example, in the “Customer: Ctry‑Spec. Enh.” area where your screenshot comes from) as part of ongoing master data maintenance, not necessarily during initial migration. The Help for supplier certificates shows this general pattern: certificate details can be maintained in business partner master data and then used in checks and processes. [help.sap.com]
This means you are not blocked from going live just because there is no certificate data today.

5. Project / governance considerations

Because this is country‑specific tax master data, it’s worth:
  • Aligning with the India tax and order‑to‑cash process owners during Explore/Fit‑to‑Standard to clarify whether TCS certificate handling is in scope at all for this project (and if yes, for which customers). 
  • Documenting in your Data Migration Concept that:
    • The “Certificate Details” sheet is structurally mandatory.
    • No TCS certificates exist as of now for the in‑scope customers, so the sheet is delivered empty in the migration template.
    • The business has accepted that certificate details will be maintained later if and when they are needed.

Recommended way forward (step‑by‑step)

  1. Confirm with the customer’s India tax/business owner that no TCS certificates exist for the in‑scope customers.
  2. Leave “Certificate Details” present but empty (no data rows) in your customer migration template. Keep all headers unchanged.
  3. Run a test migration in your test tenant:
    • If the object loads without complaining about that sheet, you’re good to proceed with the same approach in productive migration.
    • If you get a specific error saying the sheet requires data rows, capture the exact message and review whether it is:
      • A strict system requirement, or
      • A behavior that should be clarified with SAP via Community / documentation feedback.
  4. Plan for later maintenance of TCS certificates in the Fiori app if the customer later obtains them.

Sources

  • SAP Help – general behavior of migration templates (“some sheets are mandatory, some are optional”). [help.sap.com]
  • SAP Help – generic guidance for migrating data with the Migrate Your Data app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com], [help.sap.com]
  • SAP Help – maintaining business partner certificate details in master data (supplier example, conceptually analogous). [help.sap.com]
AI transparency note
Note: This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance and then aligned with available SAP Help documentation. Please validate the approach in your own tenant and against the official documentation for your specific release.

If this helps, please mark the answer accordingly. Thank you.

entry.title: The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To.
entry.content:
Somewhere in your organisation, there is an integration landscape that is costing you more than it should, moving slower than the business demands, and carrying more risk than anyone has fully quantified. It probably spans more than one middleware platform, perhaps several. It almost certainly lacks complete documentation. And the team maintaining it is spending the majority of its time keeping the lights on rather than delivering anything new.

This is not a niche problem. It is the default state of enterprise integration in 2025. Decades of organic growth, acquisitions, departmental autonomy, and pragmatic decisions made under project pressure have produced landscapes of extraordinary complexity and extraordinary cost. Ask any CIO how many integration platforms are actively running in their organisation and the answer is rarely one. More typically it is three, four, or five: SAP PI/PO handling core SAP connectivity, MuleSoft managing API integrations, a legacy webMethods instance still processing B2B traffic, Boomi picked up through an acquisition, and perhaps a remnant BizTalk deployment that nobody has found the budget to retire.

The Multi-Platform Problem: Why Fragmentation Costs More Than Anyone Realises

Running multiple integration platforms simultaneously is rarely a deliberate strategic choice. It is almost always the accumulated result of individual decisions that each seemed reasonable at the time. But the cumulative effect, technically and commercially is severe, and it compounds with every passing year that the landscape is left in its fragmented state.

The Technical Cost of Fragmentation

Each platform in a multi-middleware landscape brings its own runtime, its own design-time tooling, its own monitoring stack, its own error handling patterns, and its own deployment and release process. The consequence is a set of technical problems that do not appear on any single platform’s licence invoice but are very real in their impact on the integration team and on the business it serves.

  • No single pane of glass : When an end-to-end business process fails, an order that never made it to the warehouse, a payment that did not reach the finance system, the operations team must investigate across multiple monitoring tools, each with different log formats, different retention windows, and different levels of observability. What should be a five-minute investigation becomes a 90-minute cross-platform triage exercise.
  • Incompatible security and governance models : Credential management, access policies, encryption standards, and transport protocols are defined separately on every platform. Applying a new security policy consistently across a fragmented landscape requires parallel effort on every system and creates gaps where inconsistencies go undetected. Audit and compliance exercises that should surface clean evidence instead expose a patchwork of different controls applied to different degrees.
  • Tribal knowledge and platform silos : Deep expertise on any one middleware platform takes years to build. An organisation running four platforms must maintain four pools of specialist knowledge, often held by individuals rather than teams. The departure of one expert can leave entire parts of the integration landscape effectively unsupported. And because interfaces on different platforms were built by different teams using different patterns, cross-platform consistency in design standards, error handling, and documentation is rarely achieved.
  • No coherent high-availability story : Failover, disaster recovery, and planned maintenance management must be solved separately for every platform. Each has different failover mechanisms, different RPO and RTO characteristics, and different operational runbooks. The result is an integration estate where resilience is patchy and inconsistent - high for some interfaces, untested or absent for others.

The Commercial Cost of Fragmentation

The financial consequences of a fragmented integration landscape are rarely visible in a single budget line which is precisely why they persist. They are distributed across licence costs, infrastructure costs, personnel costs, and the operational overhead of managing complexity. When Tarento models the full Total Cost of Ownership for a typical multi-platform landscape, the numbers are consistently eye-opening.

  • Duplicate licence and support costs : Each middleware platform carries its own annual licence, its own vendor support contract, and its own renewal negotiation cycle. Organisations paying MuleSoft licence fees while also holding SAP PI/PO maintenance contracts and already covered for SAP Integration Suite under their RISE or GROW subscription are in the uncomfortable position of paying three times for integration capability they could consolidate onto one platform. Tarento’s ROI Calculator consistently shows annual licence and support savings of hundreds of thousands to millions of pounds once consolidation is modelled against the full current-state cost base.
  • Multiplied infrastructure and operations overhead : On-premise and hybrid middleware platforms require infrastructure to run: servers, patching cycles, DR environments, and the engineering time to maintain them. Cloud platforms require their own tenants, their own environment management, and their own upgrade planning. Multiplied across three, four, or five platforms, the infrastructure and operational overhead is a significant and recurring cost that consolidation can largely eliminate.
  • The hidden cost of slower delivery : New integration requirements, connecting a new SaaS application, enabling a new supplier, automating a new business process must be scoped and delivered separately on whichever platform happens to own that domain. Routing decisions are made not by strategic logic but by which team has capacity. Reusable assets built on one platform cannot be leveraged on another. The net effect is that a fragmented landscape systematically slows down the pace at which the business can evolve its digital operations, with a real but difficult-to-quantify opportunity cost attached to every delayed integration project.

 

The Multi-Platform Tax: Every integration platform an organisation runs above one adds licence cost, infrastructure overhead, operational complexity, and staffing expense. Tarento’s TCO modelling of multi-platform landscapes consistently shows that the annual cost of running three or more middleware platforms, when all cost components are properly accounted for is 2 to 4 times higher than the equivalent cost on a single, consolidated cloud-native platform. This is the multi-platform tax. And most organisations have been paying it for years without fully recognising it.

 

 

Why Leading Enterprises Are Consolidating Now

The multi-platform problem is not new. What has changed is that the pressure to solve it has become acute at exactly the same moment that a credible consolidation target has emerged and the tools to execute the migration have matured to the point where the economics are compelling.

Global enterprise leaders, from Nordic consumer goods conglomerates and global port equipment manufacturers to Asian diversified conglomerates and global specialty chemicals companies, are executing integration consolidation programmes not because it is strategically tidy, but because the business case is undeniable. Reduce the licence estate. Eliminate the infrastructure overhead. Build a single, expert integration team rather than four specialist silos. Adopt a unified monitoring and governance model. And position the integration layer to support the AI-driven business transformation agenda that every board is now being held accountable for delivering.

SAP Integration Suite on BTP has become the consolidation target of choice for SAP-centric enterprises. It is not simply that it is included in RISE and GROW subscriptions, though that matters significantly for the financial case. It is that the platform genuinely delivers the breadth of integration capability that was previously split across multiple tools: A2A, B2B/EDI, API management, event-driven integration through SAP Event Mesh, and pre-built connectivity to thousands of applications through the integration content catalogue. Consolidating onto SAP Integration Suite does not require the business to accept reduced capability. In most cases, it expands it.

The question is no longer whether to consolidate. The question is how to do it without the budget spiralling, the timeline extending indefinitely, and the business being disrupted in the process. That is exactly the question that Tarento has been answering, in production, for some of the world’s most demanding enterprises.

We have migrated landscapes spanning 150 to more than 2,500+ interfaces. We have consolidated platforms ranging from SAP PI/PO and MuleSoft to webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, Informatica and others -  all onto SAP Integration Suite. And we have done it faster, cheaper, and with lower risk than any purely manual approach could achieve. The reason is iVolve: Tarento’s AI-powered integration modernisation programme, built from the ground up around automation at every stage of the journey.

Today, we are launching a five-part blog series that takes you inside the iVolve approach; from the strategic decisions that set a programme up for success, to the automation tools that make the numbers work, to the operational capabilities that keep a modernised landscape running at enterprise grade. Whether you are a CTO building the case for a board that wants to see ROI, an integration architect scoping a migration you know needs to happen, or an IT operations leader looking for optimisations, this series is written for you.

 

 

The State of Enterprise Integration in 2026

Enterprise integration is at an inflection point. Three forces are converging to make the status quo untenable for most large organisations.

Force 1: The End-of-Support Clock

SAP PI/PO mainstream maintenance ends in 2027. SAP CPI Neo is being deprecated in favour of Cloud Foundry. webMethods is mid-transition following its acquisition by IBM. MuleSoft licensing costs continue to escalate. BizTalk has been in extended support for years. For organisations on any of these platforms, the migration question is not a strategic preference, it is a deadline. Every quarter of inaction is a quarter closer to running unsupported, unlicensed, or unviable software at the heart of business operations.

Force 2: The Economics of Cloud-Native Integration

SAP Integration Suite on BTP is included as part of most SAP RISE and GROW subscriptions. Organisations standardising on S/4HANA, SAP Cloud, or the broader SAP BTP ecosystem are already paying for world-class integration capability, and leaving it unused while continuing to pay separate licences for legacy middleware. The TCO differential, when properly modelled, is striking. Tarento’s customers have seen financial projections showing returns of 200% to 350% over ten years, with break-even periods as short as 12 to 18 months. The economics of staying put are simply no longer defensible.

Force 3: The Velocity Imperative

Business units are no longer willing to wait 12 weeks for a new integration to be delivered by an overwhelmed integration team managing 500 legacy interfaces. The AI-driven business transformation programmes that CEOs are committing to require an integration layer that can move at the pace of the business; real-time, event-driven, API-enabled, and capable of connecting new SaaS applications in days rather than months. Legacy middleware, by design, cannot deliver this. Cloud-native integration platforms, properly implemented and operated, can.

An Emerging Context : AI and Integration

The iPaaS market is experiencing explosive growth, projected to reach $79.38 billion by 2030, driven by AI's transformative impact on integration platforms. AI is revolutionizing iPaaS by enabling intelligent automation, self-healing infrastructure, and proactive monitoring, shifting platforms from reactive data movers to intelligent hubs that predict failures and optimize workflows.

Leading vendors like Boomi, Workato, Informatica, and SAP are rapidly integrating AI-powered assistants and low-code/no-code capabilities to democratize integration development, allowing citizen developers to build automation without deep technical expertise. A critical driver is real-time data access: agentic AI applications demand seamless, context-aware data retrieval across enterprise systems, making AI-powered integration platforms essential for next-generation AI workflows.

However, organizations face emerging security challenges, by 2028, 25% of enterprise breaches may stem from AI agent abuse, making governance and built-in guardrails paramount. The shift from "SaaS with AI features" to "AI-native SaaS" in 2026 signals that platforms embedding AI natively from inception will dominate, while legacy integration approaches become obsolete in supporting modern AI-driven digital transformation.

SAP leads the 2025 Gartner Magic Quadrant for iPaaS (fifth consecutive year) with generative AI capabilities enabling workflow automation, API anomaly detection, and intelligent data mapping. The platform offers 10,500+ pre-built artifacts and 250+ third-party adapters, with 95% customer recommendation rates. SAP positions Integration Suite as the critical infrastructure for agentic AI applications requiring secure, real-time API access across enterprise systems.

However, there continues to be an ongoing debate about SAP's strength primarily being with existing SAP customers seeking to consolidate iPaaS and connect SAP with non-SAP systems, rather than as a standalone iPaaS competitor. Personally, I feel that SAP has closed the gap significantly and is now a serious contender, with an added advantage of having the edge when it comes to operating in a SAP centric ecosystem.

 

Why Migration Programmes Fail, and Why iVolve Is Different

Integration migration projects have a poor reputation for good reason. They routinely overrun timelines, exhaust budgets, and fail to deliver the operational improvements that justified the investment. The pattern is familiar: an optimistic initial estimate that did not account for the full complexity of the landscape, a manual migration process that scales badly, a testing phase that consumes months because test cases had to be written from scratch, and a post-go-live environment that lacks the governance and tooling to be properly operated.

iVolve was purpose-built to break this pattern. Tarento is an SAP Validated Partner Use Case holder and an SAP Migration Factory Partner. iVolve is available on the SAP Store. The iVolve Framework replaces the manual, error-prone, effort-intensive stages of a migration with automation, AI, and proven accelerators achieving automation factors of 30% to 95% depending on the source platform, compressing programme timelines from years to months, and delivering a post-migration landscape that is actively governed, monitored, and resilient from day one.

 

What’s in the Series

Over five blogs, we cover every stage of the integration modernisation journey, the various iVolve tools and accelerators including approaches that make each stage faster, more certain, and more valuable.

 

Part A

The Integration Modernisation Imperative

The strategic case for integration modernisation, why SAP Integration Suite is the right target platform, and how the iVolve lifecycle framework addresses every stage from legacy discovery to operational excellence.

Part B

Making the Case

How to build a compelling, finance-grade business case for SAP Integration Suite consolidation including TCO modelling, NPV, IRR, and the ROI Calculator tool that has helped enterprises from global manufacturers to diversified conglomerates unlock investment approval.

Part C

Automate Everything

A deep dive into the iVolve automation engine; the Discovery Agent, Migration Automator, the MINT App for Neo-to-Cloud Foundry migrations, and the TestEase Bundle with real programme benchmarks showing automation factors of 30% to 95% across every major source platform.

Part D

Beyond Go-Live

How the iVolve OpEase Suite; transport governance, enhanced monitoring, AI-powered alerts, and the iFlow AI Advisory App ensures a modernised SAP Integration Suite landscape operates with the rigour and intelligence that enterprise operations demand.

Part E

When Downtime Is Not an Option

Why organisations that run real-time business operations on SAP Integration Suite need a proven failover strategy, and how the OneFailover Application delivers near-zero downtime through automated sync, orchestrated cutover, and continuous readiness scoring.

 

Who Should Read This Series

These blogs are written for the leaders and practitioners who are closest to the integration challenge, and the ones who will be held accountable for solving it.

  • CTOs and IT leadership who need to make a defensible case for integration modernisation investment and want the financial framework and customer evidence to do it
  • Enterprise and solution architects who are planning a migration, want to understand the automation tooling in depth, and need to scope a programme that will actually deliver on its estimates
  • Integration operations teams who are managing a live SAP Integration Suite landscape and want to understand the tools available to improve resilience, governance, and operational intelligence
  • Finance and procurement leaders who will be asked to approve a migration investment and want to understand the financial model, the risk profile, and the track record behind the proposal

Start Now: If you are running SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, or any legacy middleware and are ready to understand what a modern, automated migration to SAP Integration Suite would actually look like for your landscape, start with Blog 1 and 3. If you need the financial case first, go straight to Blog 2. And if you are already on SAP Integration Suite and are wondering whether your landscape is as resilient as it needs to be, Blogs 4 & 5 are for you. 

To explore this further, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part A : The Integration Modernization Imperative
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To.

Enterprise integration sits at the heart of every digital transformation. Yet for many organisations, the integration layer built incrementally over decades on platforms like SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, BizTalk, or Boomi has quietly become one of the most significant barriers to change. Hundreds or even thousands of interfaces, fragmented across multiple middleware platforms, maintained by specialists, and carrying the institutional memory of decades of business logic.

The question is no longer whether to modernise and consolidate. It is how to do it without breaking the business in the process.

The Fragmented Integration Landscape: A Universal Problem

Most enterprises did not plan to end up with multiple integration platforms. They grew through acquisitions, departmental autonomy, and vendor-led decisions. The result is a sprawling landscape of technical debt:

  • Multiple middleware platforms each with their own licensing costs, upgrade cycles, and specialist skills requirements
  • Integration logic scattered across hundreds or thousands of discrete artefacts with little or no documentation
  • Aging on-premise infrastructure that creates bottlenecks for cloud adoption and real-time processing
  • Integration teams spending the majority of their time on maintenance rather than value-adding innovation

 

Real-World Context

According to Tarento's iVolve programme data, customers commonly present with 150 to over 2,500+ interfaces requiring modernisation across platforms including SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, Informatica, and BizTalk.

 

 

Why SAP Integration Suite Is the Strategic Target

SAP Integration Suite on BTP represents a fundamentally different kind of platform. It is cloud-native, continuously updated, AI-enabled, and deeply integrated with SAP's broader business technology ecosystem. For organisations standardising on SAP S/4HANA or the SAP cloud portfolio, it provides a natural home for integration logic, reducing the dependency surface and aligning the integration layer to the pace of the business.

The platform offers pre-built content, open connectivity, B2B/EDI capabilities, API management, and event-driven integration, all under a single subscription. The operational economics of eliminating separate middleware licences, infrastructure, and specialist teams are substantial.

We have also expanded on the emerging AI context in the previous blog and how SAP is positioning itself as a leading integration platform.

 

The iVolve Approach: Accelerators, AI, and Automation Across the Lifecycle

Tarento's iVolve programme was built around a single insight: migration projects fail or overrun not because of a lack of skilled consultants, but because of the sheer volume of manual, repetitive work involved. Every interface needs to be discovered, assessed, redesigned, migrated, documented, and tested. Do this manually for say 500 interfaces and you have a multi-year programme with significant risk.

iVolve introduces automation and AI at every stage of the journey:

 

Feature

iVolve Capability

Legacy Discovery

Discovery Agent automatically scans and catalogues the source landscape, classifying interfaces by complexity and migration readiness

Platform Strategy

Platform Recommendation Tool and Vector Sprint consulting engagement define the target architecture and phased roadmap

Business Case

ROI Calculator provides financial modelling i.e. TCO, annual savings, NPV, IRR  to anchor the investment decision

Migration

Migration Automator drives 30–80% automation of interface migration depending on source platform, with iVolve AI Agentic Workbench handling complex artefacts

Testing

TestEase Bundle provides automated functional, B2B, and coverage testing, eliminating manual test scripting

DevOps & Ops

OpEase Suite provides transport governance, near-zero downtime failover, and AI-powered monitoring

 

 

The Four Pillars of a Successful Integration Modernisation

Tarento's experience across more than 15 completed modernisation programmes, spanning from 150 to over 2,500+ interfaces  has crystallised a four-pillar delivery methodology:

  • Discover with Confidence : You cannot plan what you cannot see. The Discovery pillar uses the iVolve Discovery Agent to produce a complete inventory of the legacy landscape, including interface complexity scoring and a migration approach document. This document gives customers a genuine Go / No-Go decision point before committing to migration spend.
  • Migrate with Automation : The Migration pillar deploys the iVolve Migration Automator against the source platform, generating migrated iFlows at scale. The iFlow AI Advisory App ensures each flow follows appropriate target patterns; not just a literal lift-and-shift, but a modernisation-aware transformation.
  • Test with Assurance : The Testing pillar applies the TestEase Bundle to validate every migrated interface functionally, with automated B2B testing and test coverage analysis. Customers can choose the level of automated versus manual testing engagement based on their internal preferences and risk appetite.
  • Operate with Resilience : The Integration Operations pillar, powered by the OpEase Suite, ensures the post-go-live landscape is actively governed, monitored, and resilient. The OneFailover application provides near-zero downtime failover for critical landscapes. Enhanced monitoring and transport governance complete the picture.

 

 

The Promise: Faster, Cheaper, Lower Risk

The iVolve approach delivers on three dimensions simultaneously. Programmes complete faster because automation removes the long tail of manual migration effort. They cost less because the automation factor directly reduces person-hours, and iVolve requires no additional licensing beyond the SAP BTP subscription the customer is already committing to. And they carry lower risk because automated migration reduces human error, automated testing catches regressions early, and the Discovery Agent means no nasty surprises mid-programme.

In the next blog in this series, we will examine how to build the financial and strategic case for integration modernisation including the specific ROI metrics and business case components that have helped customers from a leading Nordic consumer goods conglomerate to a global beer manufacturer and a major port equipment and automation company unlock budget and executive approval.

 
Know More & Get Started: Drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part B : Making the Case (Figuring Out RoI, TCO and Other Metrices)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is a continuation of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read Part A : The Integration Modernization Imperative before continuing.

Every integration modernisation programme starts the same way: a technically compelling argument that struggles to gain traction in the boardroom. IT leaders can describe the fragility of the landscape, the rising cost of maintenance, the risk of falling off support. But without a clear financial narrative and without numbers that connect to budget lines, decisions stall.

This blog examines how organisations can build a rigorous, defensible business case for integration consolidation onto SAP Integration Suite, and how Tarento's ROI Calculator accelerator makes that process faster and more credible.

 

Why Business Cases for Integration Migrations Fail

The typical integration modernisation business case fails for one or more of three reasons: it cannot quantify current costs with confidence, it cannot model future costs credibly, and it cannot answer the timing question, "When does the organisation break even, and what does delay cost every month?".

These are solvable problems. But they require a structured analytical approach, not a back-of-envelope estimate.

 

The Total Cost of Ownership Framework

The starting point for any credible business case is a complete picture of what the current integration landscape actually costs. This is almost always higher than initial estimates because costs are distributed across multiple categories:

  • Software licensing - Middleware platform licences, which for commercial platforms like MuleSoft, webMethods or others can be substantial
  • Infrastructure - On-premise server costs including hardware refresh cycles, data centre, and support contracts
  • Personnel - The integration specialists who maintain the landscape, including contractor rates for niche skills
  • Operations -  Monitoring tooling, incident management, on-call costs, and planned maintenance effort
  • Upgrade and compliance risk - The hidden cost of deferred upgrades and the growing risk premium of running out-of-support software

 

 

 

The ROI Calculator: Tarento's ROI Calculator aggregates all of these cost dimensions into a Total Cost of Ownership model for the current state, then calculates the equivalent TCO for a post-migration SAP Integration Suite landscape.

 

The Financial Metrics That Drive Decisions

The iVolve ROI Calculator generates six financial outputs, each designed to answer a specific question that arises in a capital approval process. Together, they build a complete and progressive argument i.e. from “How much do we save?” to “Why is this the best use of this budget?”

Annual Savings : The Recurring Dividend

Annual Savings is the fundamental measure of ongoing value: how much the organisation stops paying every year once migration is complete. Calculated as Current Platform TCO minus SAP BTP annual cost, it aggregates licence savings, infrastructure consolidation, reduced operational overhead, and freed personnel capacity. In Tarento’s multi-platform reference scenario; for example, consolidating SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, and Boomi onto SAP Integration Suite, this figure reaches $1.06 million per year.

Break-Even Period : When the Investment Pays for Itself

Calculated as Migration Cost ÷ Annual Savings × 12, the break-even period tells decision-makers precisely when the migration investment is fully recovered through accumulated savings. It is the most universally understood capital test.

Here is an example from one of our customers. Against a $710K migration investment and $1.06M in annual savings, break-even falls at 9 months - full recovery of the investment before the end of year one. That single figure tends to resolve boardroom hesitation faster than any other number in the analysis.

3/5/10-Year ROI : The Return on Capital

ROI percentage is calculated as (Cumulative Savings − Migration Cost) ÷ Migration Cost × 100. It expresses the total return on the migration investment at standard planning horizons and is the most intuitive measure for non-finance stakeholders. In the reference scenario, the 3-year ROI reaches 346%, meaning the organisation recovers its full investment and receives more than three times that amount in additional savings within three years of completion.

The 5- and 10-year figures compound this further, underpinning the Value Multiple discussed below.

Net Present Value (NPV) : What the Future Savings Are Worth Today

ROI percentage captures the size of the return but not its timing. A migration that delivers the same total savings over three years is worth more than one that delivers it over ten, because earlier cash flows can be redeployed into the business sooner. NPV corrects for this using the time value of money.

Each year’s savings stream is discounted at 10%, reflecting the opportunity cost of capital, the return the organisation could achieve by deploying the same budget elsewhere, and the migration cost is then subtracted.

A positive NPV confirms that the migration creates genuine economic value even after accounting for the cost of capital. In a reference scenario, the 5-year NPV exceeds $3.3 million: the organisation is that much better off in today’s money terms than if it deferred the decision. NPV is the metric finance committees rely on most heavily for capital project comparison, because it normalises for timing and allows direct, like-for-like comparison between competing investment proposals.

Internal Rate of Return (IRR) : The Comparison Benchmark

IRR is the discount rate at which the NPV of the migration equals zero. In other words, the effective annual return rate the project generates on the capital invested. Finance teams use it to rank projects against each other and against the organisation’s hurdle rate (typically the weighted average cost of capital, commonly 8–12%).

For a migration with a 9-month payback and annual savings exceeding 150% of the migration cost, IRR runs well above 100%. That is a figure that clears any standard corporate hurdle rate and compares favourably against virtually any alternative capital deployment. Placing that number alongside the organisation’s cost of capital is typically the moment a finance committee moves from “interesting” to “approved.”

Value Multiple : The Executive Headline

Calculated as 10-Year Total Savings ÷ Migration Cost, the Value Multiple is the simplest executive summary: for every dollar invested in migration, how many dollars does the organisation receive back over a ten-year horizon?

A Value Multiple of 6x or higher, typical in consolidations from high-cost commercial platforms, means every dollar of migration investment returns six dollars or more over the decade. This is often the number that headlines the financial case.

 

Multi-Platform Consolidation: Where the Real Value Hides

For organisations running multiple integration platforms simultaneously, a common situation for post-merger enterprises or those that have gone through multiple vendor cycles, the business case becomes especially powerful. The ROI Calculator supports multi-platform consolidated analysis, modelling the simultaneous convergence of, for example, SAP PI/PO and MuleSoft or webMethods and Boomi onto SAP Integration Suite.

The weighted cost allocation methodology (60% cost weight, 40% integration count weight) produces a platform breakdown showing individual ROI and savings for each source platform, alongside a consolidated savings figure. Migration phasing can be modelled to sequence platforms based on complexity and dependencies, turning an overwhelming consolidation into a manageable roadmap.

 

Three Stakeholder Perspectives, One Calculator

Different stakeholders need different things from a business case. The iVolve ROI Calculator is designed to address all three simultaneously:

  • C-Suite: Transforms complex platform decisions into clear financial metrics i.e. ROI percentage, value multiple, and the cost of delay
  • IT Leadership: Provides technical validation of platform modernisation with effort quantification - interface counts, migration phases, resource requirements
  • Finance Teams: Delivers transparent cost models suitable for capital expenditure approval, with defensible TCO, TCD and RoI calculations

 

The Opportunity Cost of Delay

One of the most powerful elements of the business case is often the monthly opportunity cost of inaction. If the migration delivers for e.g. €88,000 per month in recurring savings, every month of delay represents €88,000 of avoidable cost. Over a six-month decision cycle, that is over half a million euros of savings foregone.
