This reframes the investment decision. The question is not whether the organisation can afford to migrate; it is whether it can afford to wait.

 

Customer Example: Global Retail & Asian Conglomerate: At a global retail company and a major Asian diversified conglomerate, ROI insights were used as a catalyst to unblock stalled transformation conversations. Economic clarity replaced hesitation, enabling confident early directional calls.

 

From Business Case to Approved Programme

The ROI Calculator is the starting point of Tarento's customer engagement, typically deployed in a 2-3 week Business Case and ROI Calculation engagement that precedes the formal Discovery phase. By the time the Discovery Agent has completed its assessment and produced the Migration Approach Document, the financial case is already in the hands of the decision-makers.

Customers receive a Go / No-Go decision point with full financial visibility; the migration cost, the annual savings, the break-even date, and the 10-year value. This is the foundation on which integration modernisation programmes are approved and funded.

In the next blog, we examine how to evaluate the RoI of moving and consolidating the enterprise integration landscape on SAP Integration Suite, specifically how iVolve's automation tools deliver on the financial case by slashing the time and cost of discovery, migration, and testing.

 
Know More & Get Started: Drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com. We can help you evaluate the RoI for your enterprise landscape, and if applicable develop the business case for an integration consolidation.

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part C : Automate Everything
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read iVolve Series | Part B : Making the Case before continuing.

The business case has been approved. The ROI model has convinced the finance committee. Now comes the hard part: delivering on the numbers. Traditional integration migration projects have a poor record here. Timelines slip, effort estimates prove optimistic, and the promised savings evaporate into cost overruns. iVolve was built specifically to close this gap; by replacing manual, error-prone processes with automation at every stage.

 

The Automation Imperative

Consider what a manual migration of 500 interfaces looks like. Each interface must be analysed, documented, recreated on the target platform, adapted for target patterns, tested, and signed off. Even at a generous pace of two interfaces per consultant per day, that is 250 person-days of migration effort alone before testing, before documentation, before rework.

iVolve's Migration Automator changes this equation fundamentally. The migration automator is a multi platform integration consolidation solution. 

While SAP PI/PO migrations remains the main usecase for most customers, the iVolve Migration Automator is built as a true multi-source consolidation engine. Organisations running MuleSoft, webMethods, Boomi, BizTalk, Workato or Informatica iPaaS can feed their existing landscape directly into the same Automator pipeline, with platform-specific extractors and transformation templates handling the proprietary artefact formats, adapter configurations, and mapping constructs of each source. 

The DSL architecture (detailed in the following sections) is what makes the Automator the consolidation tool for heterogeneous landscapes: A single integration modernisation programme can, say for example, simultaneously retire MuleSoft licences, decommission an on-premise webMethods estate, and migrate inherited Boomi integrations from an acquisition, along with the existing SAP PO integration landscape all converging onto one strategic target platform.

The result is not just a faster migration but is the technical foundation that makes enterprise-wide integration consolidation commercially viable at scale.

 Figure: iVolve-supported source platforms and the Automated Discovery & Migration path to SAP BTP Integration Suite

The automation factor, the proportion of interface migration work handled without manual intervention ranges from 30% to 80% depending on source platform:

 

Feature

iVolve Capability

SAP PI/PO → SAP IS

70–80% automation : The highest factor, reflecting deep platform knowledge

MuleSoft → SAP IS

55–65% automation : Covering the majority of standard integration patterns

Boomi → SAP IS

40–50% automation : Strong coverage of common Boomi artefact types

webMethods → SAP IS

30–40% automation : Effective for A2A and B2B/EDI patterns

BizTalk → SAP IS

30–40% automation : With additional manual handling for complex orchestrations

Workato → SAP IS

50–60% automation : Strong coverage of recipe-based flows and standard connector patterns; complex event triggers, lookup tables and custom connectors addressed by the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench (Initial estimate)

Informatica iPaaS → SAP IS

30–40% automation : Effective for common mapping and ETL-style flows; complex PowerCenter and IICS transformation objects handled by the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench (Beta release, initial estimate)

Neo → Cloud Foundry

Up to 95% automation via the MINT App : Near-complete automation for CPI migration

These automation factors translate directly into programme cost savings. A 70% automation factor on a 500-interface SAP PI/PO migration means the equivalent manual effort is reduced to that of a 150-interface programme. The remainder of the migration effort is generated by the automator, with consultants focusing on review, customisation, and edge cases rather than repetitive conversion work.

 

Why SAP Integration Suite, and Why Migration Is Harder Than It Looks

Before examining how iVolve automates the migration journey, it is worth understanding why SAP Integration Suite has become the consolidation target of choice for large enterprises — and why migrating to it from platforms like MuleSoft, Boomi, or webMethods is technically demanding enough to require a purpose-built automation approach.

The Case for SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite is not merely a like-for-like replacement for legacy middleware. For enterprises running SAP S/4HANA, SAP ECC, or the broader SAP cloud portfolio, it is the native integration platform: pre-wired, pre-certified, and continuously maintained for SAP-to-SAP and SAP-to-third-party connectivity in a way that no third-party middleware can replicate. Its integration content catalogue ships with thousands of pre-built, SAP-certified integration flows for common business scenarios like order-to-cash, procure-to-pay, hire-to-retire etc that organisations on MuleSoft or Boomi must build and maintain from scratch.

Beyond SAP-native connectivity, SAP Integration Suite delivers a genuinely unified integration capability across A2A, B2B/EDI, API management, event-driven integration through SAP Event Mesh, and open connector frameworks, capabilities that on competing platforms typically require separate licensed products and separate operational teams. For organisations on RISE with SAP or GROW with SAP, Integration Suite is included in the subscription, making the commercial case for consolidation straightforward once the full TCO picture is properly modelled. And as SAP's strategic integration platform, it benefits from continuous investment, a growing pre-built content library, and deep alignment with SAP's AI and BTP roadmap in a way that third-party middleware vendors cannot match for SAP-centric landscapes.

The challenge is that arriving on SAP Integration Suite from a legacy platform is not a straightforward technical exercise. Every source platform has its own design paradigm, its own transformation language, its own adapter model, and its own concept of what an integration flow is. The distance between how MuleSoft, Boomi, Biztalk or webMethods express integration logic and how SAP Integration Suite expects that logic to be structured is significant; and bridging it manually, interface by interface, is where migration programmes traditionally consume the most time and generate the most risk.

The Platform Translation Problem

To understand the scale of the translation problem, consider the specific technical gap between MuleSoft and SAP Integration Suite. MuleSoft uses DataWeave as its transformation language; a powerful, purpose-built expression language for data transformation and mapping that is entirely proprietary to the Anypoint platform. SAP Integration Suite uses Groovy scripts and XSLT for equivalent transformation logic. These are fundamentally different languages with different syntax, different data type handling, and different approaches to mapping construction. A MuleSoft landscape with hundreds of flows may contain thousands of individual DataWeave transformations. Manually rewriting each one into Groovy or XSLT, then validating the output against the original behaviour, is the kind of work that consumes months of specialist effort and generates significant regression risk.

The problem is not unique to MuleSoft. Boomi uses its own Process Property and Map Function model for transformations. webMethods uses XSLT and its proprietary flow service constructs. BizTalk uses XSLT maps with functoids. Each represents a different design vocabulary that must be read, interpreted, and translated into the iFlow palette of SAP Integration Suite - not just syntactically, but semantically, preserving the intended business logic of the original flow in a form that is both functionally correct and architecturally aligned with SAP IS best practices.

 

What Every Source Platform Brings to the Migration Table

MuleSoft: DataWeave transformation language, Anypoint Connectors, flow-based design with Sources/Processors/Sinks, MEL expressions, and proprietary error handling scopes

Boomi: Process canvas with Shapes (connectors, maps, decision, notify), Map Functions for transformations, Process Properties for parameter management, and connector-specific operation configurations

webMethods: Flow Services with proprietary step types (INVOKE, BRANCH, LOOP, MAP), IS Documents as data structures, TN (Trading Networks) for B2B, and pipeline-based data flow

BizTalk: Orchestration using .NET-compiled Orchestration Designer, XSLT maps with functoids, Receive/Send Ports with adapter configurations, and MessageBox-based routing

 

How iVolve Solves the Translation Problem: One Schema, Any Source

The architectural breakthrough at the heart of iVolve’s migration capability is the iVolve DSL (Domain Specific Language), a universal canonical format into which any source platform’s integration logic is normalised before translation to SAP Integration Suite. Rather than building a direct, bespoke converter for each source-to-target combination, iVolve’s Extractor Layer reads the source platform’s artefacts - flow definitions, transformation scripts, adapter configurations, routing rules, error handling constructs, and represents them in a single, well-defined intermediate schema.

The Transformation Layer then converts this canonical representation into SAP Integration Suite artefacts: iFlows, Groovy scripts, XSLT mappings, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Script Collections using the iVolve Integration Template Definitions that encode SAP IS best practices directly into the code generation logic. The result is not just a functional migration. It is a migration that produces SAP IS iFlows structured the way SAP expects and recommends, with appropriate error handling, logging, externalised parameters, and flow patterns applied consistently across every migrated interface.

Critically, iVolve AI is integrated throughout this process. The AI layer increases accuracy in identifying the most appropriate SAP IS flow components and patterns for each source construct at runtime, handling cases where a direct structural mapping does not exist and intelligent interpretation of intent is required. This is the distinction between mechanical translation and intelligent migration: iVolve does not just convert syntax, it interprets the purpose of each source component and selects the best SAP IS equivalent from the iFlow palette.

From DataWeave to Groovy: Practical Example of Intelligent Code Conversion in Practice

The DataWeave-to-Groovy conversion is one of the most technically demanding aspects of a MuleSoft migration and one of the clearest demonstrations of what iVolve’s automation engine can do. DataWeave is a richly expressive, functional transformation language. A typical DataWeave script for a JSON-to-XML order transformation might combine pattern matching, conditional logic, array operations, date formatting, namespace declarations, and custom function definitions — all in DataWeave’s own idiomatic style.

iVolve’s AI-Native Integration DSL captures the semantic intent of the DataWeave script, what data is being read, what transformation is being applied, what output structure is being produced, and generates the equivalent Groovy script for SAP Integration Suite. The iVolve AI engine, drawing on its Integration Best Practices Knowledge Graph and SAP ISAM literature, ensures that the generated Groovy code follows SAP IS coding standards: correct use of the Message object, proper body and header handling, appropriate use of the SAP IS Groovy API for accessing exchange properties, and clean exception handling patterns. The output is not raw, unstructured Groovy that happens to replicate the DataWeave logic, it is SAP IS-idiomatic Groovy that an SAP integration developer would recognise as well-written, maintainable code.

The same logic applies to XSLT generation from Boomi Map Functions, to the conversion of webMethods INVOKE steps into SAP IS Receiver Channel calls, and to the translation of BizTalk functoid-based maps into SAP IS Message Mapping artefacts. In each case, iVolve reads the source construct, interprets its intent through the iVolve DSL canonical representation, and generates the most appropriate SAP IS equivalent, automatically, at scale, across hundreds of interfaces simultaneously.

Component-by-Component: How Every Legacy Element Finds Its SAP IS Home

iVolve’s component mapping is not a flat one-to-one lookup. It is an intelligent, context-aware interpretation that considers the source component’s function, its position in the flow, its relationship to adjacent components, and the overall integration pattern being expressed. The Migration Automator’s Integration Pattern Parser reads the source flow architecture and maps it to the most appropriate SAP IS palette construct, drawing on iVolve’s library of SAP IS design patterns and best-practice templates.

Some illustrative examples of how this works in practice:

Best-Practice Alignment Built Into the Output

What distinguishes iVolve’s output from a mechanical conversion tool is that SAP Integration Suite best practices are baked into the code generation templates themselves. The iFlow AI Advisory App,  informed by SAP’s Integration Architecture and Methodology (ISAM) literature and Tarento’s own Integration Best Practices Knowledge Graph, guides the choice of target iFlow pattern for each migrated interface before the automator generates the artefact. This means that every iFlow produced by iVolve reflects the correct SAP IS approach, not just a working translation. The application also accelerates the design phase, by guiding integration architects and developers on the right integration pattern of choice.

In practice, this means: adapter configurations are externalised as iFlow parameters from the outset, enabling environment portability without manual rework. Error handling is implemented using SAP IS exception subprocesses with structured logging, rather than embedded try-catch constructs that vary between developers. Message content is manipulated through the correct SAP IS step types; Content Modifier, Filter, Converter - rather than overloaded Groovy scripts that perform multiple functions. Adapters are selected based on the best-practice connectivity pattern for the target endpoint, not simply the closest structural equivalent to the source. And where the source platform used a proprietary pattern that has no direct SAP IS equivalent, such as MuleSoft’s Pub/Sub messaging or Boomi’s low-latency listener model, iVolve applies the appropriate SAP Event Mesh or queue-based pattern from the SAP IS integration content catalogue.

For the integration teams who will maintain the resulting landscape, this matters enormously. A migration that produces functionally correct but architecturally inconsistent iFlows creates a different kind of technical debt, one that will cost time and effort to address in operations. iVolve’s approach ensures that the migrated landscape does not simply replicate the functional behaviour of the original; it lands on SAP Integration Suite as a well-structured, consistently governed, maintainable integration estate that the organisation can run, extend, and evolve with confidence.

 

How to Approach a Modernisation Programme with iVolve? 

Phase 1: Discovery Agent : See Everything Before You Commit

Migration programmes that run into trouble almost always share a common root cause: the source landscape was not fully understood at the outset. Interfaces that appeared simple in the inventory turn out to contain complex Java mappings or custom adapter modules. Dependencies between flows are not documented. Artefacts that were believed to be in use have been dormant for years.

The iVolve Discovery Agent eliminates these surprises. It connects to the source middleware platform like SAP PI/PO, MuleSoft, webMethods, including several others and systematically:

  • Inventories all integration artefacts with full metadata capture
  • Assigns a complexity score to each interface based on mapping complexity, adapter types, and dependencies
  • Identifies artefacts that are unused or candidates for decommissioning rather than migration i.e. a significant cost reduction opportunity
  • Produces a Migration Approach Document that serves as the programme blueprint, including migration plan, effort estimates, and risk assessment
  • Interface recommendations to leverage equivalent SAP Standard integration content, especially for SAP Cloud Solutions.
Accelerate Your Discovery: Tarento has completed discovery engagements across landscapes ranging from 1,000 to 3,500+ interfaces, across SAP PO, Informatica, and BizTalk platforms, each producing a Migration Approach Document within 1-2 weeks.

Phase 2: Migration Automator : iFlows at Scale

With the Discovery complete and the approach document signed off, the Migration Automator is deployed against the source landscape. The automator reads the source artefacts and generates target iFlows for SAP Integration Suite, applying:

  • Appropriate iFlow patterns recommended by the iFlow AI Advisory App, not just a literal copy of source logic but a modernisation-aware transformation
  • Custom logging and error handling frameworks consistent with target platform standards
  • Dynamic Adapter modifications during migration based on target architecture requirements
  • Split functionality to enable Pub-Sub models and add queuing where the target architecture calls for it
  • Reusable integration flow templates to ensure consistency across the migrated landscape

For complex artefacts that the automator cannot handle natively i.e. Java mappings, custom adapter modules, unusual database connections, the iVolve AI Agentic Workbench provides targeted AI-assisted transformation, producing target-specific artefacts or API scaffolds. This aids in further reduction of manual efforts.

The Documentation Agent runs in parallel, generating technical documentation for every iVolve-produced iFlow automatically. This addresses one of the most persistent pain points of legacy integration landscapes: documentation that is absent, outdated, or impenetrable.

Phase 2b: Neo to Cloud Foundry - The MINT App Special Case

For organisations running SAP CPI on the Neo environment, the migration path to SAP Integration Suite on Cloud Foundry (BTP CF) is a distinct technical challenge, and one that iVolve addresses with a purpose-built accelerator: the MINT App (Migration Intelligence Tool). While the main Migration Automator handles cross-platform migrations from legacy middleware, MINT is specifically engineered for the CPI Neo-to-CF transition, and it achieves the highest automation factor in the entire iVolve portfolio: up to 95%.

This matters because the Neo environment is being deprecated by SAP, making the move to Cloud Foundry a mandatory, time-sensitive migration for any organisation that built its integration estate on CPI Neo. Without tooling, this migration involves manually recreating every iFlow, reconfiguring adapters and security artefacts, and retesting every interface, a labour-intensive exercise that consumes months of skilled resource. MINT transforms this into a near-automated, zero-noise transition.

What MINT Does: Five Core Capabilities

  • Discovery & Assessment : MINT automatically scans the current CPI Neo environment and builds a complete inventory of iFlows, connections, and dependencies. This provides a clear baseline of what exists today and precisely what needs attention for the migration, reducing the discovery effort by up to 40-50% compared to a manual assessment exercise.
  • Migration Blueprint : MINT produces structured compatibility reports that identify which artefacts are ready for direct migration to BTP CF and which require adjustment. This blueprint eliminates guesswork from the programme plan and ensures that effort estimates are grounded in a genuine analysis of the landscape rather than informed assumptions.
  • One-Click Migration Accelerators : This is where MINT’s 95% automation factor is realised. Built-in migration accelerators automate the conversion of iFlows and configurations that would otherwise require days of manual rework per interface. The migration effort that would traditionally occupy a team of consultants for months is compressed into a structured, automated execution, drastically cutting project timelines and resource requirements while preserving integration logic fidelity.
  • Event Mesh Enablement : MINT does not just replicate the Neo landscape on Cloud Foundry, it opens the door to future-ready architecture. Migration paths supported by MINT include enablement for SAP Event Mesh and event-driven integration models, allowing organisations to modernise not just their runtime environment but their integration architecture patterns, moving from point-to-point towards scalable, event-driven designs that are responsive to real-time business events across the enterprise.
  • Progress Tracking Dashboard : A visual dashboard provides end-to-end visibility into migration progress, blockers, and success metrics throughout the programme. Stakeholders and programme managers can track milestones and monitor completion rates in real time, ensuring the project stays on schedule with complete transparency, giving leadership confidence at every stage of the transition.

What the Numbers Say

MINT delivers up to 95% automation for Neo-to-Cloud Foundry migrations, the highest automation factor across the entire iVolve portfolio. Discovery effort is reduced by 40-50%, migration effort by up to 75%, and post-migration operational costs by up to 30%. The result is a zero-noise transition: interfaces move, the business keeps running, and the integration team emerges with a modern, cloud-native landscape on SAP BTP Integration Suite.

 

With the Neo-to-CF migration complete and the wider landscape modernisation underway, the next critical phase is ensuring that every migrated interface works exactly as intended. That is the job of the TestEase Bundle.

Phase 3: TestEase : Automated Testing as a First-Class Citizen

Testing is where migration projects historically consume disproportionate time and cost. Creating test cases, preparing test payloads, executing tests, analysing results, and retesting after fixes; all of this was manual, slow, and dependent on business involvement at inconvenient times.

 The TestEase Bundle automates this across multiple testing dimensions:

  • Functional Testing : TestEase automatically generates test suites, test cases, and payloads from SAP business documents. End-to-end message flow validation traces both technical and functional acknowledgments. Tests are reusable, payloads are captured at each lifecycle event and can be replayed for regression testing.
  • B2B / EDI Testing : Trading partner configuration testing validates sender and receiver ID mapping, message types, document standards, and partner-specific transformations. Real-time B2B execution monitoring provides step-by-step traceability for every event and transformation.
  • Test Coverage Analysis : The TestCoverage feature within TestEase provides enterprise-grade test coverage analysis with intelligent path detection and visualisation, giving teams confidence that the migrated landscape has been comprehensively validated, not just spot-checked.

 

iVolve vs. SAP's Native Tool: Unlike SAP's native migration tool, iVolve supports both Lift & Shift and Customisation, includes integrated automated testing, supports non-SAP middleware discovery, and generates migration approach documentation and delivers many more capabilities, all as standard.

 

The Compound Effect: Faster, Cheaper, More Reliable

The combined effect of automated discovery, automated migration, and automated testing is not merely additive - it is multiplicative. Programmes that would have taken 18 months complete in 8. Migrations that would have required 30 consultants require 10. Test phases that would have consumed 3 months complete in 3 weeks.

Tarento's successful programmes demonstrates predictability and consistency. These are not exceptional outcomes; they are the reliable result of applying the iVolve automation framework at scale.

 In the next blog of this series, we move beyond the migration itself to examine how iVolve's OpEase Suite ensures the post-live landscape operates with the resilience, governance, and intelligence that a modern enterprise integration platform demands.

 

Eager to Get Started: Ready to see the automation in action? Tarento offers a Discovery and Migration Pilot engagement to demonstrate iVolve's automation capability against a sample of your actual interfaces before you committing to a full programme.
Lets connect. Drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com.

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part D : Beyond Go-Live
entry.content:
Note: This blog is a continuation of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read iVolve Series | Part C: Automate Everything before continuing.

Go-live is not the end of an integration modernisation programme. It is the beginning of a new operating model. For many organisations, the transition from legacy middleware to SAP Integration Suite on BTP represents the first time their integration landscape has been managed with cloud-native DevOps practices i.e. continuous deployment, automated governance, proactive monitoring, and genuine high availability.

The challenge is that SAP Integration Suite, while a powerful platform, does not come with enterprise-grade DevOps tooling out of the box. Organisations that invest in a migration without investing in the operational layer often find themselves replicating the operational debt of the legacy landscape on a newer platform.

Tarento's OpEase Suite was built to close this gap. This blog examines the four key components and how they work together to deliver a resilient, well-governed, AI-enhanced integration operations environment.

 

OneFailover : Near-Zero Downtime for Critical Landscapes

For large integration landscapes handling business-critical transactions, planned and unplanned downtime is not just an IT inconvenience; it is a business risk with measurable financial impact. SAP Integration Suite maintenance windows, regional incidents, or platform upgrades can require traffic to be rerouted to a standby environment. Without tooling, this is a manual, error-prone process that takes hours and relies on precise coordination across multiple teams.

OneFailover provides an operational layer that standardises and automates this process entirely.

How OneFailover Works

OneFailover continuously maintains a failover tenant or runtime in a 'ready state' fully synchronised with the primary environment, tested, and prepared for immediate cutover. The core capabilities span several dimensions:

  • Sync & Orchestration: Full-scope synchronisation of iFlows, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Scripts across tenants, with dependency-aware ordering and group/sub-group priorities
  • Failover Control: Group-based methodology with deployment prioritisation and manual listener cutover, using OneFailover Profiles to map primary-to-failover configurations
  • Backup & Restore: GitHub-based, artefact-level versioning with selective restore capability and overwrite guardrails
  • Runtime Awareness: Edge Cell and multi-runtime/tenant awareness, with a persistence layer to inventory iFlows and tenant metadata
  • Observability: Operational Status and Monitoring dashboards, Readiness scores, activity and execution logs, and throughput optimisations
  • Security: User Credentials, Global Variables, Number Ranges, and Access Policies included in sync and backup, with secret-safe metadata handling

     

OneFailover integrates with enterprise routing patterns including Azure Traffic Manager, internal DNS solutions like F5 or Citrix NetScaler, and GitHub for version control, fitting naturally into existing enterprise infrastructure.

 

When to Use OneFailover

OneFailover is relevant across four operational scenarios: planned maintenance windows where manual coordination effort needs to be eliminated; regional resiliency programmes where a failover posture for critical interfaces must be maintained continuously; peak events where failover needs to be pre-staged for traffic surges; and compliance and audit requirements where evidence of DR readiness and traceable execution history is needed.

 

iFlow AI Advisory App : Design Intelligence for Dev Ease

The iFlow AI Advisory App addresses a challenge that emerges as integration teams start developing new flows and extending migrated ones on SAP Integration Suite: without deep platform expertise, developers default to familiar patterns from legacy platforms that are not optimal on BTP.

The iFlow AI Advisory App provides automated design recommendations, guiding integration teams to the most appropriate iFlow patterns for each use case. Rather than waiting for an architecture review to flag suboptimal designs, the advisory capability operates at development time, preventing technical debt from accumulating in the new landscape before it has even started.

This capability is particularly valuable for organisations that are extending their SAP Integration Suite footprint beyond the migrated interfaces into net-new integration development, ensuring that the same automation-first, pattern-led approach that characterised the migration carries through into ongoing development.

 

Transport Management & Governance Tool

Governance of the integration landscape, managing the lifecycle of iFlows from development through QA to production, is a critical operational capability that is easy to underestimate. Without structured transport management, teams resort to manual deployment procedures, version confusion, and the risk of deploying untested changes to production.

The iVolve Transport Management and Governance Tool provides the operational rigour that enterprise integration landscapes require:

  • Full transport management capabilities with controlled promotion from development through QA to production
  • Artefact comparison and version management -> compare versions before deployment to understand exactly what is changing
  • Easy access to adapter configuration details across the landscape
  • Externalised parameter management for environment-specific configurations

 

Enhanced Monitoring and AI-Powered Operations

SAP Integration Suite's native monitoring provides a solid baseline, but enterprise operations teams consistently find they need capabilities that go further particularly around error management, reporting, and proactive intelligence.

Enhanced Monitoring Tool

Built on SAP standards for seamless integration, the Enhanced Monitoring Tool provides full monitoring reports with downloadable error outputs for efficient debugging. Its restart functionality and searchable interface  by sender, receiver, message type, application ID, date, time, and statistical aggregates, enables operations teams to move from reactive firefighting to proactive management.

AI-Powered Alert Notifications

iVolve's AI-enhanced notification capability leverages SAP BTP's Integration Suite AI Adapter to enrich alert emails with GenAI-generated recommendations, giving operations teams not just the notification of a failure, but context-aware guidance on likely causes and resolution steps. This is the beginning of a shift from monitoring to intelligence-assisted operations.

Periodic Monitoring AI Agent

Looking ahead to the 2026 roadmap, Tarento is building out an autonomous Periodic Monitoring Agent that will automate routine manual monitoring activities using the iVolve AI platform, removing the operational overhead of scheduled checks and exception reviews from the integration operations team entirely.

 

The OpEase Suite as a Competitive Advantage

 Organisations that deploy the full OpEase Suite alongside their SAP Integration Suite migration achieve something qualitatively different from those that treat DevOps as an afterthought. They have a resilient, governed, intelligence-enhanced integration operations model from day one of go-live. Their operations teams are not firefighting  but are managing by exception, with tooling that surfaces the information they need and automates the responses they would otherwise need to execute manually.

 

 

Bringing It All Together: The Full iVolve Journey

Across this series, we have traced the complete arc of integration modernisation with iVolve; from the strategic imperative and business case, through automated discovery, migration and testing, to the operational excellence of the OpEase Suite. In the next and final blog of this series, we will deep dive into OneFailOver Application and how it helps enterprises architect and manage high-resiliency, and take control over the HA/Failover process in their organisations.

Across the series, the consistent thread has been automation: reducing the manual effort, the human error, and the programme risk that have historically made integration modernisation programmes difficult to justify and difficult to deliver.

Tarento’s iVolve programme is the product of deep SAP integration expertise, proprietary tooling built and refined over dozens of programmes, and a genuine commitment to delivering the ROI that customers calculate before they commit. 

 

Take the Next Step: iVolve is available on the SAP Store. Tarento is an SAP Validated Partner Use Case (VPUC) holder and SAP Migration Factory Partner. To start a Discovery engagement or run an ROI analysis for your landscape, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com.

entry.title: iVolve Series | Part E : When Downtime Is Not an Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is part of The iVolve Series | Your Integration Estate Is Holding You Back. Well, It Doesn’t Have To. Read the previous blog in this series, iVolve Series | Part D : Beyond Go-Live before continuing.

It is 11:47 PM. An online retailer’s Black Friday weekend promotion has just gone live. Orders are flooding in at three times the normal rate. Every order triggers a cascade of integration events; stock reservation, payment confirmation, warehouse dispatch, carrier booking, customer notification. All of this runs through SAP Integration Suite. And at 11:52 PM, comes an announcement of an emergency platform maintenance window. The primary tenant will be unavailable for the next 45 minutes.

For organisations without a failover strategy, this is a crisis. For organisations running OneFailover, it is a non-event. Traffic is rerouted to the failover tenant in seconds. The integration landscape keeps running. And the end customers never notice. Seamless as seamless can be!

This blog examines why integration resilience is a board-level concern for any enterprise that relies on SAP Integration Suite for real-time business operations and how Tarento’s OneFailover application transforms a complex, manual, high-risk process into a repeatable, automated, and auditable programme capability.

 

The Integration Layer Is the Business

For a previous generation of enterprises, integration middleware was infrastructure; important, but not business-critical in the way that customer-facing systems were. That distinction has collapsed. For any organisation that has consolidated its integration estate onto SAP Integration Suite, the platform is now the nervous system of real-time business operations.

Consider the industries where this is most acute. A large e-commerce retailer processes tens of thousands of orders per hour during peak periods; every order is an integration event. A global manufacturer runs real-time production planning and supplier collaboration through integration flows; a 30-minute outage ripples into production delays, penalty clauses, and customer escalations. A financial services firm processes payment instructions and regulatory reporting through integration; downtime is a compliance exposure, not just an operational inconvenience. A healthcare provider coordinates patient data flows, bed management, and pharmacy dispensing through its integration platform; unavailability can directly affect patient outcomes.

For all of these organisations, the question of what happens when SAP Integration Suite is unavailable - whether for a planned maintenance window, a regional incident, or a capacity event is not a theoretical IT risk. It is a live business continuity question that demands a credible, tested answer.

 

The Downtime Problem Is More Complex Than It Looks

SAP Integration Suite is a managed cloud service. This brings enormous benefits - Continuous updates, elastic scaling, no infrastructure management overhead. But it also means that maintenance is scheduled by SAP, not by the enterprise. Planned maintenance windows are notified in advance but are not negotiable. And unplanned incidents, while rare on a well-operated cloud platform, cannot be eliminated entirely.

The naïve response is to simply provision a second tenant and manually copy everything across when needed. In practice, this approach fails in three ways.

First, the synchronisation problem: a large integration landscape may contain hundreds of iFlows, message mappings, value mappings, scripts, security artefacts, and configuration parameters. Keeping a secondary tenant in sync with all of these manually is a significant ongoing operational burden, one that is frequently deprioritised until it is too late.

Second, the execution problem: when an incident occurs or a maintenance window opens, switching traffic to a secondary tenant requires precise, sequenced steps across multiple systems - DNS or traffic manager changes, iFlow activation, listener cutover, and verification. Doing this under pressure, manually, from a runbook, at 2 AM, creates unacceptable risk of human error.

Third, the governance problem: many regulated industries require documented, auditable evidence of disaster recovery capability. A manual failover process that lives in a shared document and has never been tested in a controlled way does not satisfy this requirement.

 

The Three Failure Modes of Manual Failover

Synchronisation drift means the failover tenant is never truly ready. Execution risk means the switchover itself is the most dangerous moment. Governance gaps mean the organisation cannot demonstrate readiness to auditors or regulators. OneFailover addresses all three simultaneously.

 

 

Introducing OneFailover: An Operational Layer for Integration Resilience

OneFailover is Tarento’s purpose-built application for SAP Integration Suite tenant resilience — available on the SAP Store and used by enterprises running large, mission-critical integration landscapes. It was formerly known as Unified Failover Management and has evolved into a comprehensive operational platform that addresses every dimension of the failover challenge: continuous synchronisation, controlled cutover, backup and recovery, observability, and governance.

The core design principle is that failover should never be an emergency response. It should be a routine, well-rehearsed capability that an operations team can invoke with confidence, whether for a scheduled maintenance window, a regional resiliency exercise, or an actual incident. OneFailover is built around that principle from the ground up.

 

The Six Pillars of OneFailover

 Pillar 1: Continuous Sync and Orchestration

OneFailover maintains a full-scope, continuously synchronised replica of the primary tenant across all artefact types: iFlows, Message Mappings, Value Mappings, and Scripts. Synchronisation is dependency-aware, artefacts that depend on each other are always moved together, in the correct sequence, preventing partial deployments that would cause failures. Group and sub-group priorities allow integration teams to define which business domains and interface clusters are synchronised first, ensuring the most critical processes are always in the highest state of readiness on the failover tenant. Single-side or both-side deployment options give teams flexibility in how they manage the synchronisation cadence.

Pillar 2: Failover Control and Cutover Execution

When the moment of cutover arrives, planned or unplanned, OneFailover provides deterministic, orchestrated execution. The group-based methodology ensures that interface clusters are activated on the failover tenant in the correct business-aware sequence: order management before warehouse execution, payment before fulfilment. OneFailover Profiles capture the complete primary-to-failover mapping configuration, including Git targets, so that the same cutover procedure can be repeated identically every time - in a drill, in a maintenance window, or in a genuine incident. An admin lock prevents simultaneous actions by multiple operators during a cutover, eliminating the risk of conflicting changes in a high-pressure situation. Manual listener cutover ensures that human confirmation is required at the point of traffic switching, maintaining control at the most consequential moment.

The cutover integrates with enterprise routing infrastructure. For inbound traffic switching, OneFailover works with Azure Traffic Manager in a priority-based setup, or with internal DNS solutions such as F5 or Citrix NetScaler, fitting into the network architecture that the enterprise already operates rather than requiring new infrastructure to be introduced.

Pillar 3: Backup, Restore and Version Control

OneFailover provides GitHub-based, artefact-level versioning for the integration landscape. Every iFlow, mapping, and configuration is versioned individually, enabling selective restore with overwrite guardrails that prevent accidental rollbacks. Failover configurations, the OneFailover Profiles, can be exported and imported, enabling them to be maintained, version-controlled, and shared across teams. This creates a complete audit trail of the integration landscape’s state over time, supporting change management, incident investigation, and regulatory compliance requirements.

Pillar 4: Multi-Runtime and Multi-Tenant Awareness

Large enterprise integration landscapes are not homogeneous. They typically span multiple SAP Integration Suite tenants, multiple runtimes including Cloud Foundry and Edge Integration Cells for sensitive on-premise data, and multiple business domains with different criticality levels and ownership. OneFailover is designed for this complexity. Its persistence layer maintains a complete inventory of iFlows and tenant metadata across the full landscape, providing a single point of control and visibility regardless of how many tenants and runtimes are in scope. Edge Cell awareness means that even hybrid landscapes  with interfaces running on-premise for data sovereignty or latency reasons, are fully included in the failover posture.

Pillar 5: Observability and Operational Dashboards

Knowing that a failover tenant exists is not the same as knowing that it is ready. OneFailover provides continuous Readiness Scores for each tenant and runtime, a quantified measure of how current the failover environment is relative to the primary, how recently it was tested, and whether any synchronisation gaps or configuration discrepancies exist. Operational Status and Monitoring dashboards give integration operations teams real-time visibility into the state of both primary and failover environments, with activity and execution logs that provide a complete record of every action taken. Throughput optimisations ensure that synchronisation activity does not create performance overhead on the primary tenant during business hours.

Pillar 6: Security, Compliance and Secret Safety

Integration landscapes contain sensitive security artefacts: User Credentials, Global Variables, Number Ranges, and Access Policies. OneFailover includes all of these in its synchronisation and backup scope, ensuring that the failover tenant is not just functionally complete but operationally ready to process real transactions. Security is maintained through SAP BTP Identity Authentication Service-powered authentication and strict secret-safe metadata handling: credentials and sensitive values are referenced by hash or reference only, never stored in plain text in version control. Strong guardrails prevent accidental exposure of production credentials during failover operations.

 

Why SAP’s Standard Capabilities Are Not Enough: The Case for OneFailover

A common assumption when enterprises first deploy SAP Integration Suite is that high availability and disaster recovery are simply included, that because SAP is a managed cloud service, the resilience problem is solved by default. This assumption is partially correct and significantly incomplete. SAP Integration Suite does provide genuine, well-engineered high availability within a single region through multi-availability zone redundancy.

For most routine operational scenarios, the above is sufficient. But for enterprises that require cross-regional disaster recovery, planned maintenance execution without service interruption, automated failover with deterministic cutover sequences, and the kind of audit-ready, governance-controlled resilience posture that regulated industries must demonstrate, SAP’s native capabilities leave substantial gaps that OneFailover is specifically engineered to fill.

The table below maps each dimension of enterprise integration resilience against what SAP provides natively and what OneFailover adds, making explicit where the standard platform ends and where OneFailover begins.

The pattern across every dimension in this table is consistent: SAP provides the foundation, and OneFailover provides the operational layer that transforms that foundation into a fully governed, automated, and enterprise-grade resilience posture. These are capabilities that matter most when a regional incident, a regulatory audit, or a peak trading event puts real pressure on the integration landscape. Treating them as optional is, in effect, a decision to accept that risk rather than manage it.

For integration teams making the case internally for OneFailover investment, this table is often the most effective starting point: it places SAP’s own standard offering alongside OneFailover’s capabilities, making the gap visible and the value proposition concrete across every dimension that leadership, operations, and compliance stakeholders care about.

 

Planning for the Four Scenarios That Matter

OneFailover’s capabilities map directly onto the four operational scenarios where integration resilience is genuinely tested:

Scenario 1: Planned Maintenance Windows

SAP notifies maintenance windows in advance, giving integration teams time to prepare. With OneFailover, preparation is straightforward: verify the Readiness Score, run a pre-cutover sync to ensure the failover tenant reflects all recent changes, and execute the controlled cutover through the OneFailover portal. The maintenance window passes with no business disruption. When the primary tenant is restored, failback follows the same orchestrated process in reverse. What would otherwise require a team of engineers working through a complex runbook becomes a two-person operation managed through an intuitive interface.

Scenario 2: Regional Resiliency and Unplanned Incidents

For organisations with a global integration landscape, regional incidents like a cloud availability zone event, a network disruption, a regional regulatory action etc may require rerouting integration traffic to a different data centre or availability zone. The risk calculus has also shifted. Recent geopolitical conflicts most notably the US/Israel and Iran confrontation have seen data centres targeted as part of military operations, causing prolonged regional outages that no amount of within-region redundancy could mitigate. Cloud infrastructure is not insulated from the consequences of armed conflict, and organisations with dependencies in or routing through affected regions are finding that out in real time. Regional resilience is no longer a contingency for rare technical failures it is a standing operational requirement. 

OneFailover’s multi-tenant and multi-runtime awareness, combined with its integration with Azure Traffic Manager and enterprise DNS infrastructure, makes regional rerouting a controlled operation rather than a crisis response. The failover tenant in a secondary region is continuously maintained in a ready state so that when rerouting is needed, whether triggered by a scheduled maintenance window or an unplanned regional event, the tenant is already current and the cutover can be executed without scrambling to synchronise artefacts under pressure.

Scenario 3: Peak Traffic Events

For e-commerce retailers, peak trading periods i.e. Black Friday, Cyber Monday, end-of-season sales etc represent the highest-value and highest-risk windows of the year. An integration outage during a 48-hour peak event can represent millions in lost revenue and lasting reputational damage. OneFailover provides the assurance that integration teams need to approach peak periods with confidence: a fully synchronised, tested, and ready failover tenant that can absorb traffic within seconds if the primary environment comes under strain or experiences an incident. The Readiness Score dashboard gives leadership a quantified, real-time view of DR preparedness in the days and hours before a peak event goes live.

Scenario 4: Compliance, Audit and DR Certification

Regulated industries; financial services, healthcare, utilities, public sector are increasingly required to demonstrate integration resilience as part of broader IT operational resilience or business continuity frameworks. The EU’s Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA), for example, requires financial entities to test their ICT continuity measures, maintain documented recovery procedures, and demonstrate that critical third-party dependencies, including cloud platforms are covered by their resilience programmes.

OneFailover’s activity logs, execution records, Readiness Scores, and repeatable playbooks provide exactly the evidence base that internal audit, external auditors, and regulators need to see: documented DR capability, tested regularly, with traceable execution history.

 

What Enterprises Gain: The Business Value Equation

 OneFailover delivers value across four dimensions that map directly to business outcomes rather than IT metrics:

  • Lower operational risk: integration outages during maintenance windows, peak events, or incidents are eliminated as a source of business disruption. The risk is not mitigated but is engineered out of the operating model
  • Faster, repeatable failover: what previously took hours of manual coordination is reduced to minutes of portal-guided execution. The same process runs identically every time, removing the dependency on tribal knowledge and individual expertise
  • Improved governance: every action is logged, every artefact is versioned, and every failover execution is traceable. The integration landscape becomes auditable in a way it never was with legacy middleware or manual processes
  • Higher confidence: integration teams, operations leadership, and the business can approach maintenance windows and peak events knowing that the failover posture is quantified, tested, and ready

 

 The 2026 Roadmap: Making OneFailover Even Smarter

Tarento’s 2026 roadmap for OneFailover extends the platform into several high-value adjacent areas. API Management integration will bring API policies and configurations into the sync and failover scope, ensuring that API-fronted integrations maintain consistent behaviour across primary and failover environments. A Centralised Key Vault integration will further strengthen the security model for credential and secret management across tenants. Advanced Proactive Alerting will shift OneFailover from a reactive tool into a predictive one, notifying operations teams of synchronisation drift or readiness degradation before it becomes a risk. Trading Partner Management capabilities will extend the failover posture to cover B2B integration configurations, ensuring that EDI and partner-facing integrations are included in the resilience framework alongside internal A2A flows.

 

Integration Resilience as a Programme Capability, Not an Afterthought

The organisations that get the most from SAP Integration Suite are those that treat it as a strategic platform, not just infrastructure. That means investing not only in migration quality and development standards, but in the operational model that keeps the platform running at the level of reliability the business requires.

For any enterprise where integration is the backbone of real-time business operations, whether in retail, manufacturing, financial services, healthcare, or logistics - OneFailover is not an optional enhancement to a well-functioning SAP Integration Suite deployment. It is the mechanism that makes a well-functioning deployment genuinely enterprise-ready: predictable, resilient, governed, and prepared for the scenarios that matter most.

The scenario at the start of this blog; an e-retailer navigating a Friday night urgent maintenance window at peak trading, illustrates what is at stake. With OneFailover, the answer to that scenario is simple: the integration landscape keeps running. The customers keep doing what they do. The business keeps moving. And when the primary tenant comes back online, failback runs just as cleanly as failover did.

 

Get Started: OneFailover is available on the SAP Store. To see a live demonstration of failover execution and the Readiness Score dashboard, or to discuss how OneFailover fits into your SAP Integration Suite operating model, drop me a note on shabarish.nair@tarento.com.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud Customer Spotlights: Building Data Foundations for AI
entry.content:
The Need for Connected Business Data to Power Enterprise AI

Across industries, organizations are realizing that AI success starts with connected, trusted business data. But for many companies, data is still fragmented across systems, teams, and processes—making it difficult to turn AI ambition into real outcomes.

In this ongoing customer spotlight series, we highlight how leading organizations are using SAP Business Data Cloud to connect their data, enable better decision-making, and build the foundation for reliable AI. This page serves as a central hub for these stories and will be continuously updated as new customer spotlights are released.

 

Featured Customer Stories

SAF-HOLLAND

Operating in a complex manufacturing environment, SAF-HOLLAND manages data across multiple systems following years of expansion and acquisitions. By consolidating fragmented manufacturing data into a unified architecture, the company is creating the foundation required to scale AI across the business.

 

Piller Blowers & Compressors

AI is only as strong as the data behind it — and that's why Piller Blowers & Compressors GmbH is implementing a business data fabric architecture. Piller is using SAP Business Data Cloud to bring together disconnected systems and harmonize data models through a business data fabric approach — creating trusted, business-ready data products that accelerate analytics and unlock AI use cases.

 

Endress+Hauser

Real-time decisions require accurate data to be accessible at the moment action matters most. Endress+Hauser Group technicians depend on equipment lifecycle data to service and optimize customer systems in real time — but fragmented systems made it difficult. With SAP Business Data Cloud, Endress+Hauser is combining operational data dispersed across systems into a unified data fabric, enabling real-time insight and laying the groundwork for AI-driven optimization.

 

More Stories Coming Soon

This blog will continue to be updated with the latest SAP Business Data Cloud customer stories, highlighting how organizations across industries are connecting their data to drive analytics, automation, and AI innovation.

Check back regularly for new spotlights and real-world examples of how enterprises are turning connected business data into impact.

To hear more customer stories like this, join us at our upcoming virtual event: The Fabric of Data & AI https://url.sap/vlm80u

entry.title: Re: Change batch in mass
entry.content:
Dear @AlineCogaLima,

In Public Cloud does not currently standard Fiori app for mass change of batch master data. The standard app Manage Batches (F2462) allows users to search for single batches or for a range of batches and maintain batch information

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/34b021588aee0a02e10000000a44147b.html

SAP explicitly describes changing a batch record, which confirms standard manual maintenance is available, but not a separate Public Cloud mass-change application, so for bulk updates the documented Public Cloud option is the Batch API. SAP for Operations for Batch API confirms that the service supports update operations and SAP explicitly states that PATCH can be used for entities Batch and BatchPlant to change batch data. This is therefore the supported approach when many batch records need to be updated at once

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9ce855c709ce4f5c99b685903461106e/318d52d13b234c9096bfaf30a926fa78.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9ce855c709ce4f5c99b685903461106e/3d366e68d53345b4bad055ec8fe85d6e.html

Summary:

In Public Cloud there is no standard mass-change app for batches:

  • For manual maintenance, use Manage Batches (F2462)
  • For real bulk changes use the Batch API / integration approach

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Request for Guidance on Production Staging Configurat...
entry.content:
Good day @kkumara 

Thank you for your question.

In the standard Public Cloud design, production order release does not automatically create a warehouse task. The standard trigger is material staging, which is also referred to as MF60 Stage Materials for Production. So, when you stage, the system creates an outbound delivery which is then processed in the warehouse e.g., for tasks, picking and printing.

See Delivery-Based Production Supply and Introducing Warehouse Production Integration for more details.

If Stage Material for Production Order Level does not retrieve the production order, the most common Public Cloud causes are:

  • The scope item 5HO is not fully activated (you might require SAP to enable it by raising an incident).
  • Your staging step cannot create the delivery due to missing prerequisites.
  • Your MF60 behaviour has been impacted. See KBA 3567624 and KBA 2934515 for more details.

My recommendations are as follows:

  • Confirm the order is released and has component demand
  • Test staging in MF60 with a short horizon
  • Verify whether an outbound delivery is created, see KBA 2960232 for some insights
  • If you cannot execute staging under 5HO, then follow the guidance provided by KBA 3670761 and Delivery-Based Production Supply.

 To enable the process, you will require your Central Business Configuration.

  1. In Manage Your Solution, confirm you have activated the 5HO scope item for warehouse production integration , if you have and you still face this issue, then raise an incident.
  2. Ensure delivery-based production supply is the active design. This is a prerequisite if you want warehoused tasks and pick slips.
  3. Validate MF60 staging prerequisites and ensure whether staging is actually creating a delivery, and whether your scenario is aligned to the expected warehouse production setup.

 

Here is a quick guide to checks you can carry out in your system.

  • In your Manage Production Orders app, ensure the order status is Released, then confirm that components exist and quantities are required.

  • In MF60, select Production Order tab or Production order level, enter your details (Plant, Prod. Order number, requirement date range, etc.) and execute it.

  • If your you get the result “order number not retrieved or not usable for filtering” then KBA 3567624 and KBA 2934515 describe that behaviour and what your next steps should be.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Clarification on using Object grouping in Real Estate...
entry.content:
Good day @acoronado 

Thank you for your question.

What you call “Real Estate Contract” is typically treated in Public Cloud as Contract and Lease. This does not fully cover the classic RE-FX feature. In Public Cloud, the provided solution for theses contracts is CLM.

The distinction I made above is important because classic FR-FX object groups are well known in on premise but they are not consistently exposed as a first class configurable object grouping feature in the CLM Public Cloud UI. In Public Cloud, you normally achieve your requirement by structuring contract object assignments and conditions, rather than relying on an object group configuration node.

See the Blog CLM in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and the reference below for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/b7138d1ebe19469a889137bc1abd3ae5.html?locale=en-US

Question 1: How can Object Grouping be utilized in S/4HANA Public Cloud Real Estate Contracts to support this requirement?

Answer: In Public Cloud CLM, object group exists as a concept in the Object Assignments model, meaning contracts can reference object groups as an assignment type. For your case, this means:

  • If your UI explicitly allows assigning an Object Group and pricing it, you can use it.
  • If your UI does not expose Object Group maintenance and pricing cleanly, the standard, supported approach is to split the 20 units into two assignment sets and maintain two condition lines, one per set.

This will avoid custom development and works consistently.

Consult these references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3bd9b5b7e36c4628a29ffc464bf69b48/a9eec8f6185d4d8d8a6ee05a8406ade0.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/b7138d1ebe19469a889137bc1abd3ae5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/6a231842444b4448b1abcc97fe7762b7.html?locale=en-US

Question 2: Is there a standard approach to group multiple rental objects and assign a single rate per group?

Answer: Yes, the standard approach is contract structuring and not CBC customising. I recommend

  • Group A: assign 10 units
  • Group B: assign 10 units
  • Create two condition lines, each tied to its object assignment set:
    • Condition 1: rate 2,000 for Group A
    • Condition 2: rate 2,500 for Group B

CLM supports multiple object assignments and contract conditions as standard.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/b7138d1ebe19469a889137bc1abd3ae5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/6a231842444b4448b1abcc97fe7762b7.html?locale=en-US

Below are answers to your questions.

Question 3: If object grouping is supported, could you advise on the recommended configuration or process to achieve this scenario?

Answer: A recommend process that work in all Public Cloud CLM systems is:

First step: Assign objects in two sets. Open the contract, in the Object Assignments, add the first 10 units and the second 10 units (see grouping mentioned in the earlier question).

Second step: Maintain one condition per set, create a condition for Group A with 2,000 unit price and same for B with 2,500 unit price. Validate the cash flow reflects 10 x 2,000 and 10 x 2,500.

If your tenant truly exposes “Object Group” in UI, then do the following:

  1. Maintain Object groups in your rentable object master process
  2. Assign the object group to the contract (instead of individual units)
  3. Apply one condition to that object group

If you cannot find “Object Group” in your UI, do not force it through customisation. In Public Cloud, you do that with an API driven approach through Object Assignments, which is integration work rather than configuration.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3bd9b5b7e36c4628a29ffc464bf69b48/a9eec8f6185d4d8d8a6ee05a8406ade0.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Default Partner Bank and Payment methods from Supplie...
entry.content:
Good day @lakshminarayanam 

Thank you for your question.

From my understanding what you are trying to achieve are as follows:

  1. Default Payment Method on the supplier invoice from master data
  2. Auto select the supplier Partner Bank based on invoice currency when multiple bank accounts exist.

Here are some vital information:

In Public Cloud, the payment method is not copied from the supplier master record into the supplier invoice by design. The standard approach is to default payment method via the Payment Terms you use in your invoice. SSCUI 102934 is important to achieve that.

For supplier bank selection when multiple bank account exists, your invoice must carry the Partner Bank Type that identifies which supplier bank account should be used. If Partner Bank Type is bland, your invoice processing can raise warning that correspond to currencies and ensure the correct Partner Bank Type is selected or populated during invoice creation.

 

Here are direct answers to your requirement.

Requirement: “Default payment method from Supplier Master to invoice”

Answer: It is not supported. The system does not copy Payment Method from supplier master to Invoice. The supported alternative is a default via Payment terms using SSCUI 102934. See KBA 3207431 and KBA 3353780 for more insights.

Requirement: “Auto select supplier bank account base on invoice currency”

Answer: The Public Cloud does not provide fully automatic function for that. You should use Partner Bank Type to select the correct bank account on the invoice. See KBA 3696647 for more details. A best practice workaround is to encode Partner Bank Type by currency and enforce manual selection or through inbound mapping.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to join a parameterized view?
entry.content:
Hi @rhavin ,

Could you please collect more trace data and see if you can find any hints or clues from the trace?

[Enable trace]
```
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini','SYSTEM') SET
('sqltrace','user')='${DATABASE_USER_NAME}',
('sqltrace','application_user')='${APPLICATION_USER_NAME}',
('sqltrace','tracefile')= 'sqltrace_20260307.py',
('sqltrace','trace')='on',
('sqltrace','internal')= 'true',
('sqltrace','details')='all',
('sqltrace','level')='all_with_results',
('sqltrace','query_plan_trace')= 'on'
WITH RECONFIGURE;
```

[Reproduce the error]

[Disable trace]
```
ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini','SYSTEM') UNSET
('sqltrace','user'),
('sqltrace','application_user'),
('sqltrace','tracefile'),
('sqltrace','trace'),
('sqltrace','internal'),
('sqltrace','details'),
('sqltrace','level'),
('sqltrace','query_plan_trace')
WITH RECONFIGURE;
```

If you still encounter the error, could you please upload the DDL of the tables (e.g., UFD1, CUFD, etc.) and include some test data? This will help us reproduce the issue in our test system.

Best regards,
Charles

entry.title: Vibe-coding in ABAP via an MCP Server with OpenAI Codex
entry.content:
In my previous blog post I described installing an MCP Server which wraps ABAP Development Tools API, and extending it with some WRITE functionality - all conversationally with AI Agents.

I asked Perplexity to go look into GitHub, see what other MCP Servers people have built. She recommended mcp-abap-adt from fr0ster as the best all-rounder: https://github.com/fr0ster/mcp-abap-adt.

I again used an Agent in Google Antigravity to clone the repo and install it. Updated *.env configuration manually and asked the Antigravity Agent to update the configuration for OpenAI Codex, that I had installed as a desktop app on my Mac. That didn't work flawlessly and Codex (GPT-5.3) had to go through some shenanigans to make it work, but it the end he managed. He queried the MCP and found that it exposed massive 131 tools:

Nice. Good job fr0ster and team!

In the spirit of vibe-coding, I asked Codex to create a simple SALV Report. That took about 84 seconds:

Codex proactively suggested that we should add a Selection Screen. I specified the requirement, and he added the Selection Screen:

What he couldn't add were Text Elements for the Selection Screen Fields. No tool for that in the MCP - ADT API itself probably doesn't expose such functionality.

Next I inquired whether he would be able to build a full-stack Fiori app. He confidently replied "Why yes of course, I thought you'd never ask" (OK, he didn't reply exactly that, but something in that spirit). I specified an app and he proceeded to make a pig's ear of it. Probably my fault - I suspect my specification was poor. It's all about the Specification in the AI age, people! The better the Prompt, the better the result. Otherwise garbage in - garbage out.

Oh, and Context. Context Engineering is king.

And Intent for more complex tasks. But that's it, I swear.

 

So there you have it - a bit of vibe-coding, ABAP style. Joule it ain't, but beggars-choosers, I guess. Joule will have been trained on a much larger ABAP codebase. Claude, Codex and Co. can only see what's in GitHub, maybe SAP Help,  and various publicly-available documents.

Time permitting, I'll get back to the Fiori App topic (also with Claude). I have a feeling there is more to be achieved there.

entry.title: Re: Can Custom Alternative Material Logic Be Embedded in...
entry.content:
Hello @zhangtaiping

Below is my response to your follow-up questions.

Question: When using the PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST BAdI intensively, can it support highly customized logic? For example, during an MRP run, could we dynamically modify the BOM components and their required quantities for a specific product based on custom rules?

Answer: No, not in the sense of “runtime BOM substitution”. The BAdI is positioned for modifying the supply and demand list that is imported during a planning run, for example to change, add, or remove MRP elements in that list. It is not designed to rewrite the BOM explosion result and then have MRP generate a completely different component structure as if the BOM were different.

So “highly customised logic” is only feasible within the boundary of supply demand list elements but not as a replacement for BOM determination and component explosion.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/eb381da44986470cadfa80891d81fe6d.html?locale=en-US

Question: For example, during an MRP run, could we dynamically modify the BOM components and their required quantities for a specific product based on custom rules?

Answer: Based on your description I do not think this is achievable via PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST in a clean way and supported by Public Cloud. The reasons are:

  • Available stock is not something you should “fake” in MRP extension logic. It comes from stock ledgers and MRP netting. If you virtually change it, that would be inconsistent with ATP, inventory, and other follow up execution.
  • Replacing C with D and E is effectively changing the component explosion outcome. The BAdI is about the MRP element list and not about you redefining the BOM structure that produces dependent requirements.

However, the Public Cloud provides you option that stick to the clean core without you breaking the planning model.

  • You can model substitution with master data driven alternatives, so MRP net requirements and proposals are created based on available standard guidelines.
  • If your customer needs “non standard substitution”, that typically belongs in advanced planning products, which is not part of the Public Cloud MRP engine.

Note that the MRP run parameter options do not perform substitution. They only control whether components are included in planning scope. See KBA 3051894 for more insights.

Question: in what scenarios would the use of this BAdI be considered appropriate and safe in a Public Cloud environment? Are there official best practices or constraints that ensure such extensions meet business needs while remaining compliant with cloud-native governance principles?

Answer: PPH_SUPPLY_DEMAND_LIST is appropriate in Public Cloud when you use it for small, well scoped adjustment of MRP elements, such as:

  • When you want to exclude certain supply or demand elements from net requirements calculation.
  • You have the need to adjust dates or quantities of specific MRP elements based on stable rules that are determinants.
  • You want to add a custom MRP element to the list.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Intrastat Region
entry.content:
Hello @sanne_larsen

You are not missing a “mapping step”. What you are seeing is two different region concepts.

  • The SSCUI region lists (like your “insert Regions” screen) typically show administrative subdivisions/provinces. You can maintain these via SSCUI 103826 Insert Regions. See KBA 3449400 for more details.
  • Belgium Intrastat “Region” is a statistical region code that the Belgian authorities require. These are: 1=Flemish region, 2= Walloon region, 3= Brussels Capital region.

That is why your Intrastat report shows Flemish, Walloon, Brussels while the SSCUI list show provinces like Antwerpen, Limburg, East Flanders, etc.

Below is my answer to your questions:

Question: Is there any type of mapping somewhere between the different regions since they differ?

Answer: No. Intrastat for Belgium expect the statistical region code. If your plant address region is maintained with a province code, Intrastat validation will still fail because the value is not one of the accepted Intrastat region codes.

Question: do I need to add the region somewhere else then SSCUI 102191?

Answer: Yes, for Public Cloud, the safe way/path is for you to:

  • Use SSCUI 103826 (if you do not already have them) to maintain the statistical region codes as valid “Regions” for Belgium.
  • Then maintain your plant address region with one of these codes. This will enable Intrastat to automatically derive it.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Credit Memo Request to correct Debit Memo
entry.content:
Hello @Johannes_Wienho

You are right about the key constraint. In that case, the standard approach to issue a credit note for an L2 debit memo is for you to use the billing creation process and reference chain to create a billing credit memo, rather than a credit memo request that references L2. Note that creating billing documents with SD document reference is supported and this includes Credit Memo as a billing type.

BR

Chris

entry.title: Re: Unable to Create Custom Analytical Query in SAP S/4HA...
entry.content:
Hello @Octavio_Ruiz4

Given you can create in Dev with standard BR_ANALYTICS_SPECIALIST, but not in Production, the following are likely to be the source of the issue:

  • SAP_BW_BC_AQD_PC is not writeable. Most probably because the catalog has an Read Only assignment, or role write access is restricted.
  • According to KBA 3038415, key user extensibility is temporarily disabled in Production due to maintenance window.

I recommend you check in Production and verify SAP_BW_BC_AQD_PC is not assigned a Read Only in the business role and confirm whether other key user extensibility apps are also blocked, which would indicate a system wide temporary lock.

BR

Chris
